docs.title: Dnevnici veza i QSL kartice
docs.text: Svaka održana veza treba biti zapisana. To je zakonska obaveza i dio tradicije.

docs.title: Dnevnik veza (Logbook)
docs.text: Dnevnik mora sadržavati: - Datum i vrijeme (UTC). - Pozivnu oznaku korespondenta. - Frekvenciju (ili band). - Vrstu rada (Mode). - Raport (RST).

docs.title: Papirnati vs. Elektronički
docs.text: Danas većina koristi računalne programe (loggere) kao što su N1MM, DXKeeper ili Ham Radio Deluxe. Oni automatski bilježe vrijeme i frekvenciju (ako su spojeni na uređaj).

docs.title: QSL Kartice
docs.text: QSL kartica je pismena potvrda veze. To je "konačna pristojnost" radioamatera. - Papirnate QSL: Šalju se direktno poštom ili (jeftinije) preko QSL biroa nacionalnog saveza (HRS). - Elektroničke QSL: - LoTW (Logbook of The World): ARRL-ov sustav. Službeno priznat za diplome (DXCC). - eQSL: Popularan, ali manje "služben". - ClubLog: Odličan za analizu i OQRS (Online QSL Request System).

docs.title: Vrste rada (Modovi)
docs.text: Radioamateri koriste različite načine modulacije za prijenos informacija. Svaki mod ima svoje prednosti, mane i specifičan "zvuk".

docs.title: CW (Telegrafija)
docs.text: Najstarija vrsta komunikacije. Koristi Morseov kod (točke i crtice). * Prednosti: Domet (prolazi i kad se ništa drugo ne čuje), jednostavna oprema, mala širina pojasa. * Zvuk: Ritmično piskanje. * Poslušaj CW (Video)

docs.title: SSB (Single Sideband)
docs.text: Najpopularniji mod za prijenos glasa na kratkom valu (HF). Naprednija verzija AM-a koja troši manje energije. * LSB (Lower Sideband): Koristi se ispod 10 MHz (40m, 80m). * USB (Upper Sideband): Koristi se iznad 10 MHz (20m, 17m...) i na VHF/UHF-u. * Zvuk: Ljudski glas, ali zvuči kao "Donald Duck" ako niste točno na frekvenciji. * Poslušaj SSB (Video)

docs.title: FM (Frekvencijska modulacija)
docs.text: Standard za lokalne veze na VHF/UHF-u (ručne stanice). * Prednosti: Čist zvuk, bez šuma (ako je signal jak). * Mane: Traži puno širi pojas, slab domet u odnosu na SSB/CW.

docs.title: Digitalni Modovi (FT8, RTTY...)
docs.text: Računalo i radio rade zajedno. * FT8: Trenutno najpopularniji mod. Omogućuje veze s vrlo malim snagama i nečujnim signalima. Veza je automatizirana. * RTTY (Teletype): Stariji digitalni mod, zvuči kao "igrica iz 80-ih". * Zvuk FT8: Zvuči kao višestruko zavijanje. * Poslušaj FT8 (Video)

docs.title: Vodopad (Waterfall)
docs.text: Moderni uređaji i softver prikazuju signale vizualno. * X os: Frekvencija. * Y os: Vrijeme. * Boja: Jačina signala. * Omogućuje da "vidite" stanice prije nego ih čujete.

docs.title: Procedura veze (QSO)
docs.text: Održavanje veze ima svoja pravila kako bi komunikacija bila efikasna i razumljiva.

docs.title: Osnovni pojmovi
docs.text:
• CQ: Opći poziv ("Calling Any Station").
  • DE: Od (koristi se u CW-u ili digitalnim modovima).
  • K: Prelazim na prijem ("Over").
  • 73: Pozdrav ("Best Regards").

docs.title: Primjer veze (SSB)
docs.text:
1. Poziv: "CQ CQ CQ, ovo je 9A1AA, 9A1AA zove i sluša."
  2. Odgovor: "9A1AA, ovo je DL1BBB."
  3. Raport: "DL1BBB, dobar dan. Tvoj signal je 59 (pet-devet), ime je Ivan, lokator JN75. 9A1AA."
  4. Potvrda: "9A1AA, hvala Ivane. Tvoj signal je također 59, moje ime je Hans. 73!"
  5. Kraj: "Hvala Hans, 73 i čujemo se. DL1BBB de 9A1AA."

docs.title: RST Sustav
docs.text:
Raport se sastoji od 3 znamenke (za CW) ili 2 (za SSB): - R (Readability): Čitljivost (1-5). - S (Signal Strength): Jačina signala (1-9). - T (Tone): Kvaliteta tona (1-9, samo za CW).

Primjer: 599 (Savršen signal na telegrafiji).

docs.title: Q-Kodovi
docs.text: Q-kodovi su standardizirane kratice od tri slova koje počinju slovom Q. Izvorno su razvijene za telegrafiju kako bi ubrzale komunikaciju, ali se danas koriste i u govornim modovima.

docs.title: Često korišteni Q-kodovi
docs.text: Kod Pitanje Odgovor / Obavijest QRA Kako se zove vaša stanica? Ime moje stanice je... QRG Koja je moja točna frekvencija? Vaša točna frekvencija je ... kHz. QRL Jeste li zauzeti? Zauzet sam (molim ne ometajte). QRM Imate li smetnje od drugih stanica? Imam smetnje od drugih stanica (1-5). QRN Imate li atmosferske smetnje? Imam atmosferske smetnje (1-5). QRO Trebam li povećati snagu? Povećajte snagu. QRP Trebam li smanjiti snagu? Smanjite snagu. QRT Prekidate li rad? Prekidam rad. QRZ Tko me zove? Zove vas... (na ... kHz). QSB Da li moj signal varira (feding)? Vaš signal varira. QSL Možete li potvrditi prijem? Potvrđujem prijem. QSO Možete li komunicirati s...? Mogu komunicirati s... QSY Trebam li promijeniti frekvenciju? Promijenite frekvenciju na ... kHz. QTH Koja je vaša lokacija? Moja lokacija je...

docs.title: Svi Q-kodovi (Radioamaterski)
docs.text: Kod Značenje QRA Ime stanice / Adresa QRB Udaljenost između stanica QRC Tko plaća račun (komercijalno, rijetko u amaterizmu) QRD Kamo idete i odakle dolazite? QRE Kada očekujete dolazak? QRF Vraćate li se? QRG Točna frekvencija QRH Varira li moja frekvencija? QRI Ton signala (T u RST) QRJ Primate li me loše? QRK Čitljivost signala (R u RST) QRL Zauzeta frekvencija QRM Smetnje od drugih stanica QRN Atmosferske smetnje (šum) QRO Velika snaga QRP Mala snaga QRQ Šaljite brže QRR Jeste li spremni za automatski rad? QRRS SOS za radioamatere (rijetko se koristi) QRS Šaljite sporije QRT Prestanak rada QRU Imate li što za mene? QRV Spreman sam QRW Trebam li obavijestiti...? QRX Pričekajte trenutak QRY Koji sam po redu? QRZ Tko me zove? QSA Jačina signala (S u RST) QSB Feding (variranje signala) QSD Ključanje je neispravno (telegrafija) QSK Mogu li vas čuti između mojih znakova (Break-in)? QSL Potvrda prijema (kartica) QSM Trebam li ponoviti zadnju poruku? QSN Jeste li me čuli? QSO Veza (kontakt) QSP Možete li prenijeti poruku? QSQ Imate li liječnika? QSR Trebam li ponoviti poziv? QSS Koju radnu frekvenciju koristite? QST Opći poziv svim radioamaterima (poruka) QSU Trebam li slati na ovoj frekvenciji? QSV Trebam li slati seriju V (za ugađanje)? QSW Hoćete li slati na ovoj frekvenciji? QSX Slušate li na...? QSY Promjena frekvencije QSZ Trebam li slati svaku riječ dvaput? QTA Trebam li poništiti poruku? QTB Slažete li se s mojim brojanjem riječi? QTC Imate li poruke za slanje? QTH Lokacija (grad, lokator) QTR Točno vrijeme

docs.title: Propisi i Dozvole
docs.text: Ovdje možete pronaći sve informacije vezane uz zakonsku regulativu i postupak stjecanja radioamaterske dozvole.

docs.title: Sadržaj
docs.text:
• Kako postati radioamater?: Informacije o polaganju ispita za P i A razred.
  • Hrvatski propisi: Zakoni i pravilnici HAKOM-a i HRS-a.
  • Međunarodni propisi: CEPT i IARU preporuke.
  • Pozivne oznake: Struktura i dodjela pozivnih oznaka u RH.

docs.title: Korisni dokumenti
docs.text:
• Pravilnik o amaterskim radijskim komunikacijama (NN)
  • Ispitna pitanja za P razred (HRS)

docs.title: Pozivne oznake
docs.text: Pozivna oznaka (Call Sign) je jedinstveni identifikator radioamaterske postaje.

docs.title: Struktura oznake (Hrvatska)
docs.text:
Hrvatske oznake sastoje se od tri dijela:

  1. Prefiks: 9A (označava Hrvatsku).
  2. Broj: 1 - 9 (u prošlosti označavao regiju, danas nema strogo geografsko značenje, ali se često koristi: 1-Zagreb, 2-Dalmacija, 3-Istra/Primorje, 4-Slavonija...). 0 se koristi za posebne znakove.
  3. Sufiks: 1, 2 ili 3 slova.
    • 1 slovo: Klubovi s dugom tradicijom ili posebni znakovi (npr. 9A1A).
    • 2 slova: Standardne osobne oznake (npr. 9A2AA) ili klubovi.
    • 3 slova: Standardne osobne oznake (npr. 9A3AAA).

docs.title: Vrste oznaka
docs.text:
• Osobne oznake: Dodjeljuju se fizičkim osobama nakon položenog ispita.
  • Klubske oznake: Dodjeljuju se radioklubovima.
  • Posebne oznake: Za natjecanja, obljetnice ili ekspedicije (npr. 9A80Z, 9A0HQ).

docs.title: Identifikacija u radu
docs.text: Prilikom rada, radioamater mora redovito emitirati svoju pozivnu oznaku (na početku, kraju i svakih 10-15 minuta tijekom veze). Kada radite iz druge zemlje (CEPT), prefiks te zemlje stavljate ispred svoje oznake (npr. S5/9A1AA ako ste u Sloveniji). Kada radite u pokretu, dodajete sufiks /P (portable), /M (mobile) ili /MM (maritime mobile).

docs.title: Hrvatski propisi
docs.text: Radioamaterizam u Hrvatskoj reguliran je nizom zakona i pravilnika.

docs.title: 1. Zakon o elektroničkim komunikacijama
docs.text: Temeljni zakon koji uređuje uporabu radiofrekvencijskog spektra. - Link na zakon (Narodne novine)

docs.title: 2. Pravilnik o amaterskim radijskim komunikacijama
docs.text: Ovo je "biblija" za radioamatere. Definira: - Vrste dozvola i razrede. - Frekvencijska područja i snage. - Tehničke uvjete za postaje. - Način formiranja pozivnih oznaka. - Link na pravilnik (Narodne novine) (Napomena: Provjerite najnoviju verziju)

docs.title: 3. Nacionalna tablica namjene radiofrekvencijskog spektra
docs.text: Dokument koji određuje namjenu svakog dijela spektra u RH.

docs.title: Nadležna tijela
docs.text:
• HAKOM (Hrvatska regulatorna agencija za mrežne djelatnosti): Izdaje dozvole, dodjeljuje pozivne oznake i nadzire rad.
    • Web: www.hakom.hr
  • HRS (Hrvatski radioamaterski savez): Nacionalni savez koji zastupa interese radioamatera, organizira ispite i vodi QSL biro.
    • Web: www.hamradio.hr

docs.title: Kako postati radioamater?
docs.text: Put do radioamaterske dozvole vodi preko polaganja stručnog ispita. U Republici Hrvatskoj postoje dva razreda radioamatera: P (početnički) i A (napredni).

docs.title: P razred (Početnički)
docs.text: Ovo je ulazna točka u svijet radioamaterizma. - Prava: Rad na svim amaterskim frekvencijskim područjima, ali s ograničenom snagom (do 100W na KV, do 50W na UKV). - Ispit: Jednostavniji, fokusiran na osnove propisa, tehnike i operativnog rada. - Preporuka: Idealno za početnike koji žele brzo krenuti s radom.

docs.title: A razred (Napredni)
docs.text: Puni radioamaterski privilegiji. - Prava: Rad maksimalnom dozvoljenom snagom (do 1.5 kW u nekim slučajevima) i pristup svim međunarodnim privilegijama (CEPT HAREC). - Ispit: Opsežniji, uključuje dublje poznavanje elektronike i radiotehnike. - Preporuka: Za one koji žele iskoristiti puni potencijal hobija.

docs.title: Postupak polaganja ispita
docs.text:
1. Priprema:
    • Pronađite najbliži radioklub (Popis klubova). Klubovi često organiziraju tečajeve.
    • Preuzmite priručnike i pitanja s web stranice HRS-a.
  2. Prijava:
    • Ispiti se organiziraju u radioklubovima ili direktno u HRS-u.
  3. Ispit:
    • Sastoji se od pismenog dijela (test). Nema više praktičnog dijela (Morseov kod nije obavezan).

docs.title: Nakon ispita
docs.text:
Nakon uspješno položenog ispita dobivate Svjedodžbu o položenom ispitu. S njom podnosite zahtjev HAKOM-u za izdavanje Dozvole za uporabu RF spektra i dodjelu pozivne oznake.

Savjet

Najbolji način za učenje je uz mentora u radioklubu. Praktično iskustvo je neprocjenjivo!

docs.title: Međunarodni propisi
docs.text: Radioamaterizam je globalni hobi, stoga je međunarodna koordinacija ključna.

docs.title: ITU (International Telecommunication Union)
docs.text: Međunarodna telekomunikacijska unija je agencija UN-a koja koordinira korištenje radiofrekvencijskog spektra na globalnoj razini. - Radio Regulations (RR): Međunarodni pravilnik koji definira amatersku službu.

docs.title: IARU (International Amateur Radio Union)
docs.text: Međunarodna radioamaterska unija zastupa interese radioamatera pred ITU-om. - Podijeljena je na 3 regije: 1. Regija 1: Europa, Afrika, Bliski Istok, Sjeverna Azija. 2. Regija 2: Sjeverna i Južna Amerika. 3. Regija 3: Ostatak Azije, Australija, Oceanija.

docs.title: CEPT (European Conference of Postal and Telecommunications Administrations)
docs.text: Europska organizacija koja usklađuje propise. - Preporuka T/R 61-01: Omogućuje radioamaterima s CEPT licencom (u Hrvatskoj A razred) rad u drugim zemljama potpisnicama bez dodatne birokracije (do 3 mjeseca). - Preporuka T/R 61-02: Harmonizirani ispitni program (HAREC).

docs.title: Što to znači za vas?
docs.text: Ako položite A razred u Hrvatskoj, vaša dozvola je priznata u većini europskih zemalja (i mnogim drugima). Možete putovati i raditi s vašom stanicom koristeći prefiks zemlje u kojoj se nalazite (npr. DL/9A1AA).

docs.title: Resursi
docs.text: Korisni linkovi, alati i softver za radioamatere.

docs.title: Online Alati
docs.text:
• Rječnik (Kratice): Objašnjenja pojmova i kratica.
  • PSK Reporter: Karta aktivnosti digitalnih modova u stvarnom vremenu.
  • DX Summit: DX Cluster.
  • Reverse Beacon Network (RBN): Automatsko praćenje CW/RTTY signala.

docs.title: Hrvatska (HR) - Istaknuto
docs.text:
• Hrvatski Radioamaterski Savez (HRS): Krovna organizacija.
  • HAKOM: Regulatorna agencija.
  • OIV: Odašiljači i veze.

docs.title: IARU Regija 1 (Europa, Afrika, Bliski Istok)
docs.text:
• IARU Region 1: Službena stranica regije 1.
  • DARC: Njemački savez (odlični tehnički resursi).
  • RSGB: Britanski savez.

docs.title: IARU Regija 2 (Amerike)
docs.text:
• ARRL: Američki savez (najveća baza znanja).
  • RAC: Kanadski savez.

docs.title: IARU Regija 3 (Azija, Pacifik)
docs.text:
• JARL: Japanski savez.
  • WIA: Australski savez.

docs.title: Softver
docs.text:
• WSJT-X: Standard za FT8/FT4.
  • N1MM Logger+: Najpopularniji contest log.
  • Ham Radio Deluxe: Sve-u-jednom rješenje.

docs.title: Radioamaterski Rječnik (Kratice)
docs.text:
Ovdje možete pronaći objašnjenja najčešćih kratica koje koriste radioamateri.

A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | L | M | O | P | Q | R | S | T | U | V | W | X | Y | Z

docs.title: A
docs.text:
• AF (Audio Frequency): Zvučna frekvencija. Raspon frekvencija koje ljudsko uho može čuti (približno 20 Hz do 20 kHz).
  • AGC (Automatic Gain Control): Automatska regulacija pojačanja. Sklop koji održava konstantnu razinu izlaznog signala unatoč promjenama ulaznog signala.
  • ALC (Automatic Level Control): Automatska kontrola razine. Sklop u odašiljaču koji sprječava premodulaciju ili preopterećenje izlaznog stupnja.
  • AM (Amplitude Modulation): Amplitudna modulacija. Vrsta modulacije gdje se informacija prenosi promjenom amplitude nosioca.
  • APRS (Automatic Packet Reporting System): APRS (Link na EN). Sustav za prijenos podataka u stvarnom vremenu (pozicija, poruke, telemetrija) putem radija.

docs.title: B
docs.text:
• BCI (Broadcast Interference): Smetnje uzrokovane radiodifuznim postajama (npr. FM radio ili TV).
  • BFO (Beat Frequency Oscillator): BFO (Link na EN). Oscilator u prijemniku koji miješanjem s međufrekvencijom proizvodi čujan ton, nužan za prijem CW i SSB signala.
  • BK (Break-in): QSK (Link na EN). Mogućnost slušanja između vlastitih znakova tijekom odašiljanja telegrafije (Full Break-in).

docs.title: C
docs.text:
• CEPT: CEPT. Europska konferencija poštanskih i telekomunikacijskih uprava koja regulira međusobno priznavanje radioamaterskih dozvola.
  • CQ: CQ (poziv) (Link na EN). Opći poziv svim stanicama ("Calling any station").
  • CW (Continuous Wave): Telegrafija. Način komunikacije prekidanjem nosioca (Morseov kod).

docs.title: D
docs.text:
• dB (Decibel): Decibel. Logaritamska mjerna jedinica za izražavanje odnosa dviju veličina (npr. snage ili napona).
  • Dipol: Dipol antena. Najosnovnija vrsta antene koja se sastoji od dva vodiča, ukupne duljine približno pola valne duljine.
  • Dummy Load: Umjetno opterećenje (Link na EN). Otpornik (obično 50 ohma) koji se spaja na odašiljač umjesto antene za potrebe testiranja bez zračenja signala.
  • DX (Distance): DXing. Oznaka za veliku udaljenost ili vezu s udaljenom/rijetkom stanicom.

docs.title: E
docs.text:
• EME (Earth-Moon-Earth): EME komunikacija (Link na EN). Tehnika komunikacije odbijanjem radio signala od Mjeseca.
  • ERP (Effective Radiated Power): Efektivna izračena snaga. Umnožak snage dovedene na antenu i dobitka antene u odnosu na polurasni dipol.

docs.title: F
docs.text:
• FM (Frequency Modulation): Frekvencijska modulacija. Vrsta modulacije gdje se informacija prenosi promjenom frekvencije nosioca.
  • FT8: FT8 (Link na EN). Popularan digitalni način rada dizajniran za komunikaciju slabim signalima.

docs.title: G
docs.text:
• GND (Ground): Uzemljenje. Električni spoj s tlom radi sigurnosti i referentnog potencijala.
  • GP (Ground Plane): Monopol antena. Vertikalna antena (monopol) koja koristi "umjetnu zemlju" od radijala kako bi simulirala idealnu vodljivu ravninu.

docs.title: H
docs.text:
• HF (High Frequency): Kratki val. Radiofrekvencijski opseg od 3 do 30 MHz.
  • HT (Handheld Transceiver): Walkie-talkie. Mala, prijenosna radio stanica s baterijom i antenom u jednom kućištu.

docs.title: I
docs.text:
• IARU: IARU (Link na EN). Međunarodna radioamaterska unija, savez nacionalnih udruga.
  • ITU: Međunarodna telekomunikacijska unija. Agencija UN-a za informacijske i komunikacijske tehnologije.

docs.title: L
docs.text:
• LDS (Long Delayed Echo): LDE (Link na EN). Fenomen gdje se radio signal vraća pošiljatelju nakon nekoliko sekundi kašnjenja.
  • LSB (Lower Sideband): Jednobočna modulacija. Donji bočni pojas amplitudne modulacije.

docs.title: M
docs.text: • MUF (Maximum Usable Frequency): MUF (Link na EN). Najviša frekvencija koja se može koristiti za komunikaciju između dvije točke putem refleksije od ionosfere.

docs.title: O
docs.text: • OM (Old Man): Žargonski izraz za radioamatera (muškog), bez obzira na dob. Prijateljsko oslovljavanje.

docs.title: P
docs.text:
• PA (Power Amplifier): Pojačalo snage. Uređaj koji povećava snagu radio signala prije odašiljanja.
  • PEP (Peak Envelope Power): Vršna snaga ovojnice (Link na EN). Standardna mjera snage za SSB odašiljače.
  • PTT (Push To Talk): PTT (Link na EN). Tipka ili prekidač koji prebacuje uređaj s prijema na predaju.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: (Vidi Q-kodovi za detaljniji popis) - Q-kod: Q-kod. Standardizirana zbirka tročlanih kodova za komunikaciju.

docs.title: R
docs.text:
• RIT (Receiver Incremental Tuning): Funkcija koja omogućuje finu promjenu frekvencije prijema bez utjecaja na frekvenciju odašiljanja (Clarifier).
  • RST: RST kod (Link na EN). Sustav za ocjenjivanje kvalitete signala (Čitljivost, Snaga, Ton).
  • RTTY (Radioteletype): Radioteleprinter. Telekomunikacijski sustav za prijenos teksta putem radija.

docs.title: S
docs.text:
• SWR (Standing Wave Ratio): Omjer stojnih valova. Mjera prilagođenosti impedancije antene i odašiljača.
  • SSB (Single Sideband): Jednobočna modulacija. Efikasan način prijenosa glasa korištenjem samo jednog bočnog pojasa.
  • Squelch: Squelch (Link na EN). Sklop koji prigušuje šum iz zvučnika kada nema korisnog signala.

docs.title: T
docs.text:
• TVI (Television Interference): Smetnje koje radio odašiljač uzrokuje na televizijskom prijemu.
  • TX: Kratica za odašiljač (Transmitter) ili čin odašiljanja.

docs.title: U
docs.text:
• UHF (Ultra High Frequency): UHF. Radiofrekvencijski opseg od 300 MHz do 3 GHz.
  • USB (Upper Sideband): Jednobočna modulacija. Gornji bočni pojas amplitudne modulacije.
  • UTC: Koordinirano univerzalno vrijeme. Vremenski standard koji se koristi u radioamaterizmu.

docs.title: V
docs.text:
• VFO (Variable Frequency Oscillator): Oscilator promjenjive frekvencije (Link na EN). Dio radija koji omogućuje kontinuirano mijenjanje frekvencije.
  • VHF (Very High Frequency): VHF. Radiofrekvencijski opseg od 30 do 300 MHz.
  • VOX (Voice Operated Exchange): VOX (Link na EN). Sustav koji automatski uključuje odašiljač kada detektira glas.

docs.title: W
docs.text:
• WARC: WARC opsezi (Link na EN). Radioamaterski opsezi dodijeljeni 1979. godine (12m, 17m, 30m).
  • WPM (Words Per Minute): Riječi u minuti (Link na EN). Mjerna jedinica za brzinu slanja/primanja telegrafije.

docs.title: X
docs.text: • XYL (Ex-Young Lady): Žargonski izraz za suprugu radioamatera.

docs.title: Y
docs.text: • YL (Young Lady): Žargonski izraz za ženu radioamatera ili operatoricu.

docs.title: Z
docs.text: • Z (Zulu Time): Zulu vrijeme (Link na EN). Vojna/avijatičarska oznaka za UTC.

docs.title: Tehnika
docs.text: Tehnički aspekt je srž radioamaterizma. Ovdje obrađujemo sve od osnova elektronike do naprednih komunikacijskih sustava.

docs.title: Sadržaj
docs.text:
• Osnove elektronike: Otpornici, kondenzatori, zavojnice, poluvodiči.
  • Antene i vodovi: Dipoli, Yagi, vertikalke, koaksijalni kabeli.
  • Prijemnici i SDR: Software Defined Radio, RTL-SDR.
  • Primopredajnici i oprema: Kako odabrati uređaj, dodatna oprema.
  • Sigurnost u radu: Uzemljenje, zaštita od groma, RF zračenje.
  • Samogradnja (DIY): Projekti za samostalnu izradu.
  • Hamnet: High Speed Multimedia mreža.

docs.title: Antene i vodovi
docs.text: Antena je najvažniji dio svake radio stanice. Loša antena na skupom uređaju radi lošije nego dobra antena na jeftinom uređaju.

docs.title: Dipol
docs.text: Najjednostavnija i vrlo efikasna antena. * Sastoji se od dvije žice ukupne duljine \(\lambda/2\) (pola valne duljine). * Primjer za 20m (14 MHz): Ukupna duljina oko 10m. * Video: Kako radi Dipol Antena

docs.title: Yagi-Uda (Beam)
docs.text: Usmjerena antena koja pojačava signal u jednom smjeru. * Sastoji se od dipola (zračeći element), reflektora i direktora. * Koristi se za DX rad jer "fokusira" energiju (kao leća za svjetlo). * Video: Kako radi Yagi antena

docs.title: Vertikalka (Ground Plane)
docs.text: Omnidirekcijska antena (zrači u svim smjerovima). * Dobra za lokalne veze (VHF/UHF) i DX na nižim frekvencijama ako ima dobro uzemljenje (radijali). * Video: Ground Plane princip

docs.title: Koaksijalni kabel
docs.text: Najčešće korišten vod. * RG-58: Tanak, za kraće relacije i manje snage. * RG-213: Deblji, manji gubici, za veće snage. * Impedancija: Standard je 50 \(\Omega\).

docs.title: SWR (Standing Wave Ratio)
docs.text: Omjer stojnih valova. Mjera prilagođenosti antene i voda. * 1:1: Savršeno. * do 1.5:1: Odlično. * preko 2:1: Potrebno ugađanje ili korištenje tunera.

docs.title: Simulatori Antena
docs.text:
Softver za modeliranje antena omogućuje vam da "izgradite" antenu na računalu i vidite kako će raditi prije nego što odrežete ijednu žicu.

  • MMANA-GAL: Besplatan i moćan alat za modeliranje žičanih antena. Standard u hobiju.
  • 4NEC2: Napredniji NEC-2 bazirani softver za Windows.
  • Dipole Calculator: Jednostavan online kalkulator za duljine dipola.

docs.title: Osnove elektronike
docs.text: Razumijevanje elektronike je temelj za svakog radioamatera.

docs.title: Otpornik (Resistor)
docs.text:
Ograničava tok električne struje. Zamislite ga kao suženje cijevi kroz koju teče voda.

  • Jedinica: Ohm (\(\Omega\))
  • Oznaka: \(R\)
  • Primjena: Prilagodba napona, ograničavanje struje LED dioda.
  • Video: Kako radi otpornik (Engineering Mindset)

docs.title: Kondenzator (Capacitor)
docs.text:
Pohranjuje električnu energiju u električnom polju. Radi kao mala baterija koja se može jako brzo puniti i prazniti.

  • Jedinica: Farad (\(F\)) - u praksi se koriste \(\mu F\), \(nF\), \(pF\).
  • Oznaka: \(C\)
  • Primjena: Filtriranje napona (peglanje), titrajni krugovi, blokiranje istosmjerne struje.
  • Video: Kako radi kondenzator

docs.title: Zavojnica (Inductor)
docs.text:
Pohranjuje energiju u magnetskom polju. Opire se svakoj promjeni struje.

  • Jedinica: Henry (\(H\)) - najčešće \(\mu H\), \(mH\).
  • Oznaka: \(L\)
  • Primjena: Titrajni krugovi, prigušnice (blokiranje VF signala), transformatori.
  • Video: Kako radi zavojnica

docs.title: Dioda
docs.text:
Elektronički nepovratni ventil. Propušta struju samo u jednom smjeru.

  • Oznaka: \(D\)
  • Primjena: Ispravljanje izmjenične struje u istosmjernu (ispravljači), detekcija signala.
  • Video: Kako radi dioda

docs.title: Tranzistor
docs.text:
Aktivna komponenta koja može pojačavati signal ili raditi kao elektronička sklopka. Temelj je svih modernih uređaja.

  • Vrste: Bipolarni (BJT), Unipolarni (FET, MOSFET).
  • Oznaka: \(Q\) ili \(TR\)
  • Primjena: Pojačala, oscilatori, prekidači.
  • Video: Kako radi tranzistor

docs.title: Ohmov zakon
docs.text:
Temeljni zakon elektrotehnike koji opisuje odnos između napona, struje i otpora.

\[ I = \frac{U}{R} \]

Gdje je: * \(I\) = Struja (Amper - \(A\)) * \(U\) = Napon (Volt - \(V\)) * \(R\) = Otpor (Ohm - \(\Omega\))

docs.title: Snaga
docs.text:
\[ P = U \cdot I \]
  • \(P\) = Snaga (Watt - \(W\))

docs.title: Simulatori krugova
docs.text: Vizualizacija je najbolji način učenja elektronike. * Falstad Circuit Simulator: Najbolji besplatni online simulator. Pokrenite ga u pregledniku i gledajte kako struja teče. * Tinkercad Circuits: Odličan za početnike i Arduino simulacije.

docs.title: Video Lekcije (YouTube)
docs.text:
• EEVblog (Dave Jones): Dubinske analize i "teardown" videa.
  • W2AEW: Odlična objašnjenja RF elektronike i mjerenja.
  • The Signal Path: Napredna RF mjerenja i popravci.

docs.title: Članci i Tutorijali
docs.text:
• All About Circuits: Besplatan online udžbenik.
  • Electronics Tutorials: Detaljni vodiči za osnovne komponente.

docs.title: Hamnet (High Speed Multimedia)
docs.text: Hamnet je brza radioamaterska mreža bazirana na IP protokolu, koja koristi modificiranu WiFi opremu na radioamaterskim frekvencijama.

docs.title: Uvod i infrastruktura
docs.text: Što je Hamnet, kako radi i koja se oprema koristi (Ubiquiti, Mikrotik).

docs.title: Njemačka iskustva i rješenja (DAeC)
docs.text: Njemačka ima jednu od najrazvijenijih Hamnet mreža na svijetu. Ovdje ćemo prenijeti njihova iskustva i najbolje prakse.

docs.title: Primjena
docs.text:
• Povezivanje repetitora
  • Video nadzor
  • VoIP telefonija
  • Pristup serverima i servisima unutar mreže

docs.title: Prijemnici i SDR
docs.text: Prijemnik je "uši" vaše stanice. Danas razlikujemo klasične analogne prijemnike i moderan SDR (Software Defined Radio).

docs.title: Analogni (Superheterodini)
docs.text:
• Klasični dizajn s miješanjem frekvencija.
  • Odlična selektivnost, ali skuplja izrada.

docs.title: SDR (Software Defined Radio)
docs.text: Radio u kojem se većina obrade signala (modulacija, demodulacija, filtriranje) obavlja softverski. * Prednosti: Fleksibilnost, panoramski prikaz (waterfall), mogućnost dekodiranja raznih digitalnih signala. * Mane: Ovisnost o računalu, ponekad lošija dinamika kod jeftinih modela.

docs.title: Popularni SDR Uređaji
docs.text:
Ako želite jeftino ući u svijet slušanja radio valova, SDR je najbolji izbor.

  • RTL-SDR V4: Najpopularniji "entry-level" SDR. Izvorno TV tuner, modificiran za radioamatere. Cijena oko 30-40 USD.
  • Airspy HF+ Discovery: Vrhunski prijem na kratkom valu (HF). Odlična dinamika.
  • SDRplay RSP1B: Širokopojasni prijemnik (1 kHz - 2 GHz) s odličnim softverom (SDRuno).
  • HackRF One: Za napredne korisnike i eksperimentiranje (može i odašiljati malu snagu).

docs.title: Softver za SDR
docs.text:
• SDR# (SDR Sharp): Najjednostavniji za početak (Windows).
  • SDR++: Odličan open-source softver (Windows, Linux, Mac).
  • HDSDR: Lagan i brz.

docs.title: Sigurnost u radu
docs.text: Sigurnost je na prvom mjestu! Radioamateri rade s visokim naponima i elektromagnetskim zračenjem.

docs.title: Električna sigurnost
docs.text:
• Uzemljenje: Sve metalne dijelove opreme treba uzemljiti na zajedničku točku.
  • Osigurači: Koristite odgovarajuće osigurače na napajanju.
  • Antene: Nikada ne dirajte antenu dok odašiljete! Na krajevima dipola mogu se stvoriti naponi od nekoliko tisuća volti.

docs.title: Zaštita od groma
docs.text:
• Odvajanje: Najbolja zaštita je fizičko odvajanje antene od uređaja kada se ne koristi (posebno ljeti).
  • Odvodnici prenapona: Pomažu kod statičkog elektriciteta, ali ne jamče zaštitu od direktnog udara.

docs.title: RF Zračenje (PEM)
docs.text:
• Sigurne udaljenosti: Držite antene dalje od ljudi.
  • Neionizirajuće zračenje: Radio valovi ne oštećuju DNK kao rendgenske zrake, ali mogu izazvati zagrijavanje tkiva pri velikim snagama.
  • Oči: Posebno su osjetljive na zagrijavanje (UHF/mikrovalovi).

docs.title: Primopredajnici i oprema
docs.text: Izbor pravog uređaja ovisi o vašim interesima, budžetu i dostupnom prostoru.

docs.title: Ručni uređaji (Handheld / HT)
docs.text: Mali, prijenosni uređaji s vlastitom baterijom i antenom. * Najčešći opsezi: 2m (144 MHz) i 70cm (432 MHz). * Prednosti: Jeftini, mobilni, odlični za lokalnu komunikaciju i rad preko repetitora. * Nedostaci: Mala snaga (obično 5W), kratak domet s gumenom antenom.

docs.title: HF Bazne stanice (HF Base Station)
docs.text: Veći uređaji namijenjeni za radni stol u "shacku". * Opsezi: Svi KV opsezi (1.8 - 30 MHz), često i 50 MHz. * Snaga: Obično 100W. * Prednosti: Udobnost rada, velika snaga, napredne funkcije filtriranja smetnji. * Napomena: Zahtijevaju vanjsko napajanje (13.8V).

docs.title: Mobilni uređaji
docs.text: Dizajnirani za ugradnju u vozila. * Obično VHF/UHF za FM rad, ali postoje i KV mobilne stanice (npr. Icom IC-7000 serija). * Veća snaga od ručnih stanica (50W).

docs.title: Dodatna oprema
docs.text:
• Napajanje (Power Supply): Pretvara 230V AC u 13.8V DC.
  • SWR Metar: Instrument za mjerenje prilagođenosti antene.
  • Antenski Tuner (ATU): Uređaj koji prilagođava impedanciju antene uređaju.
  • Tipkalo (Key): Za telegrafiju (Morseov kod).

docs.title: Welcome to the Ham Radio Handbook
docs.text: This is the central hub for all information regarding amateur radio. Whether you are a beginner looking to pass the exam or an experienced operator seeking specific technical details, you will find everything you need here.

docs.title: What is Amateur Radio?
docs.text: Amateur radio (ham radio) is a popular hobby and service that brings people, electronics, and communication together. People use ham radio to talk across town, around the world, or even into space, all without the Internet or cell phones.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Regulations & Licensing: How to become a ham, laws, and rules.
  • Technical: Electronics, antennas, and equipment.
  • Operating: How to make a contact, modes, and procedures.
  • CB Radio: Citizens Band.
  • Activities: Contesting, SOTA, DXing.
  • Emergency Comms: Ham radio in emergencies.
  • Community: Clubs and associations.
  • Resources: Links and tools.

News

This site is under construction! Stay tuned for new content.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
Amateur radio is far more than simple communication — it is a complete ecosystem of activities that connect technology, sport, nature, competition, and an international community. Whether you are interested in mountaineering, space, competitive operating, electronics or simply long conversations with people on the other side of the planet, there is something in this hobby for you.

This chapter covers the five main activities in which Croatian amateur radio operators are particularly active.

docs.title: 🏆 Contesting
docs.text:
Organised amateur radio sport — the goal is to make as many contacts (QSOs) as possible within a set time frame. It sounds simple, but it requires operating speed, strategy, propagation knowledge, and a well-tuned station.

Contests run from a few hours to full 48-hour weekend events. Compare yourself against operators in the same category from around the world, then submit your log in the standard Cabrillo format at the end.

Why try it: Accelerates operating skills, forces you to understand propagation better, and delivers the adrenaline rush of a tight pile-up.

docs.title: 🌍 DXing — Long-Distance Operating
docs.text:
Hunting distant and rare stations — DXCC entities, of which there are approximately 340 on the entire planet. DXers monitor the DX cluster (a spotting network for rare active stations), enter pile-ups (crowds of operators calling the same rare DX station) and log confirmations via LoTW or paper QSL cards.

The DXCC award — confirming contacts with 100 different entities — is achievable by any serious DXer. The complete DXCC (all 340+) is the life's work of the most dedicated operators.

Why try it: Provides constant motivation for on-air activity — there is always a new entity to catch, a new expedition to chase, a new propagation opportunity.

docs.title: 🏔️ Portable Operations — SOTA, POTA, IOTA
docs.text:
A combined experience of amateur radio and the outdoors:

  • SOTA (Summits On The Air) — operating from mountain summits, scored by summit height
  • POTA (Parks On The Air) — activations from national parks and protected areas
  • IOTA (Islands On The Air) — operating from islands, especially popular in the Adriatic archipelago

Croatia is rich in SOTA locations (Velebit, Biokovo, Gorski Kotar, Medvednica), registered POTA parks (Plitvice, Krka, Paklenica…) and exceptionally attractive IOTA references (Palagruža is among the most sought-after EU references!).

Why try it: Combines hiking/excursions with radio, the sense of achievement from a successful activation, and QSOs with chasers worldwide who are waiting for you.

docs.title: 🛰️ Satellite Communications
docs.text:
Amateur radio operators have their own satellites in orbit — from FM repeaters accessible with a handheld radio, to advanced linear transponders for SSB and CW contacts. The ISS (International Space Station) has its own amateur radio station (NA1SS) operated manually by astronauts or automatically for APRS and SSTV.

Why try it: There is something fascinating about bouncing a signal off an object orbiting at 400 km altitude and hearing a response. The technical challenges of Doppler correction and antenna pointing make satellite work uniquely engaging.

docs.title: 🦊 Amateur Radio Direction Finding — ARDF
docs.text:
Also known as "fox hunting" — competitors use a receiver and directional antenna to locate hidden transmitters in woodland within a time limit. A combination of sport, navigation, and RF skill.

The Croatian national ARDF team has won multiple European and World medals — this is no small achievement. The discipline is especially popular among younger operators and youth (YOTA).

Why try it: For those who want physical activity combined with technology — ARDF is the perfect blend. And you will learn more about RF propagation through practical experience than from any textbook.

docs.title: Combined Activities
docs.text:
Many activities complement each other:

  • SOTA + IOTA: Operating from a mountain summit on an island (e.g. Hvar or Krk have both SOTA summits and an IOTA reference) — a rare double activation
  • SOTA + Contesting: Some operators activate SOTA summits during weekend contests — scoring points from both programmes simultaneously
  • DXing + POTA: DX hunters don't skip portable activations from POTA locations, especially if the location is unusual or rare

docs.title: Activity Calendar (General)
docs.text: Season Recommended Activities Spring (Mar–May) SOTA (without winter conditions), POTA, DXing on 10 m and 15 m (propagation improving) Summer (Jun–Aug) IOTA Contest (July), POTA, Sporadic-E VHF, ARDF competitions Autumn (Sep–Nov) CQ WW and major HF contests (!), DXing, SOTA winter bonus begins Winter (Dec–Feb) SOTA winter bonus, CW contests, DXing on 40 m and 80 m, satellites

docs.title: Where to Register and Learn More
docs.text:
• HRS (Croatian Amateur Radio Association) — hamradio.hr — Croatian activity calendar, ARDF, contests
  • SOTA — sota.org.uk — summit database, log upload
  • POTA — parksontheair.com / pota.app — park database, log upload
  • IOTA — iota-world.org — reference database, programme
  • DXCC — arrl.org/dxcc — programme and entity list

docs.title: Amateur Radio Direction Finding (ARDF)
docs.text:
Also known as "Fox Hunting", Amateur Radio Direction Finding (ARDF) is a technical sport in which competitors use a receiver and a directional antenna to locate hidden transmitters ("foxes") hidden in the field.

The discipline combines physical activity (running or fast hiking through woodland), navigation skills, and an understanding of RF propagation. Croatia has one of the most successful traditions in this discipline at both the European and world level.

docs.title: How Does a Competition Work?
docs.text:
Hidden automatic transmitters — "foxes" — take turns broadcasting short CW (Morse code) identification signals. In the standard discipline there are 5 foxes on the course, each transmitting in a cycle:

  • Fox 1: transmits for 1 minute (MOE in Morse)
  • Fox 2: transmits for 1 minute (MOI)
  • Fox 3: transmits for 1 minute (MOS)
  • Fox 4: transmits for 1 minute (MOH)
  • Fox 5: transmits for 1 minute (MO5)

After each transmission there is a 1-minute pause while the next fox transmits. Competitors must find as many foxes as possible within the time limit (typically 60–90 minutes) and return to the finish.

Why 'foxes'?

The name comes from the early competitions in the Soviet Union in the 1960s where the sport was called "охота на лис" (hunting for foxes). The name has been used worldwide ever since.

docs.title: Categories and Bands
docs.text: ARDF competitions are held on two main frequency bands, each with different technical characteristics:

docs.title: 80 m (3.5 MHz) — Classic discipline
docs.text:
• Signal propagates as a ground wave — relatively predictable behaviour
  • Easier to locate by direction finding (longer wavelengths)
  • Equipment is somewhat simpler and more robust
  • Competitors use a ferrite rod antenna (flexible loop) for direction finding
  • Terrain is usually larger (forest, hills)
  • European and World Championships on this band are generally considered the more prestigious

docs.title: 2 m (144 MHz) — More technically demanding
docs.text:
• Shorter wavelengths mean more reflections from trees and terrain
  • Direction finding is harder due to multipath signals
  • Directional antenna is a 3–5 element Yagi or an H-antenna (special DF antenna)
  • Terrain is usually smaller (urban woodland, parks)
  • Reflections produce false bearings — experience and technique are critical

docs.title: For 80 m (3.5 MHz):
docs.text: Component Description Receiver Purpose-built ARDF receiver or modified AM receiver with good AGC Ferrite loop antenna Rotating loop on ferrite core — the null (minimum signal) direction is used for DF Attenuator Essential! Near the fox the signal is too strong — attenuate to achieve a usable null Compass To record and compare bearings from multiple measurement points

docs.title: For 2 m (144 MHz):
docs.text: Component Description Receiver FM receiver with a readable S-meter or a purpose-built 2 m ARDF receiver H-antenna or 3-el. Yagi Specially designed for ARDF — directional with good F/B ratio Attenuator Again essential — attenuate heavily close to the fox Side shields Metal shielding that blocks off-axis signals — for precise null determination

docs.title: Direction Finding Technique
docs.text:
The basic technique is triangulation: 1. From one point, measure the bearing to the signal — record it on a map 2. Move 50–100 m in a direction that is not directly toward the fox 3. Take a new bearing 4. The intersection of the two lines = location of the fox

More experienced competitors use the attenuation null method — they approach the fox until the signal disappears even at maximum attenuation (the null point).

docs.title: ARDF in Croatia
docs.text:
Croatia has an exceptional tradition in ARDF. The Croatian national team has won multiple European and World medals, especially in the 80 m discipline.

Organisation: - HRS (Croatian Amateur Radio Association) — organises Croatian ARDF championships - Regular club-level competitions throughout Croatia — Zagreb, Osijek, Rijeka, Split - European/World ARDF Championship — Croatia regularly participates and places

Competition calendar: see HRS website and ARDF IARU.

docs.title: Getting Started With ARDF
docs.text:
ARDF is ideal for amateur radio operators who want to combine technical knowledge with physical activity and navigation.

Steps to get started: 1. Contact your local radio club — almost all have an ARDF section or members with knowledge 2. Attend an ARDF training session — clubs organise training where you can borrow equipment and learn technique 3. Participate in a club competition with borrowed or rented equipment 4. If you enjoy it — invest in your own ARDF receiver (€100–300)

Purpose-built ARDF receivers are available from ham radio dealers and online. Popular brands include Radi-Scan, Elecraft, and various DIY projects available in the ARDF community.

ARDF is especially good for young operators!

ARDF is particularly popular with younger operators and youth. Combining running through woodland, solving the "puzzle" of where the signal is coming from, and applying amateur radio knowledge — all in one sport! HRS actively promotes ARDF through the YOTA programme.

docs.title: ARDF and Understanding Propagation
docs.text:
ARDF is a practical school of propagation with concrete real-world examples:

  • 80 m ground wave and how it behaves in woodland vs. open terrain
  • 2 m reflections — why the signal "lies" to you when you are in a valley or near a metal fence
  • Antenna polarisation — why a horizontal antenna gives different results than vertical
  • Field antennas — improvising an antenna from available materials is a genuinely useful skill

Many experienced ARDF operators have a deep practical understanding of RF behaviour in terrain — knowledge that transfers to every other branch of amateur radio.

docs.title: Other Fox Hunting Variations
docs.text:
Beyond classic ARDF, several related activities exist:

  • Foxoring — combines orienteering with ARDF using micro-transmitters (milliwatt level), with many foxes scattered over a larger course area
  • ARDF Sprint — urban format, shorter rounds, city terrain
  • T-Hunt (Transmitter Hunt) — informal hidden transmitter hunt, popular in the USA, often done by car in suburban settings

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Antennas and Feed Lines — Yagi and DF antennas
  • Radio Wave Propagation — RF behaviour in terrain
  • Portable Operations — portable equipment relevant to ARDF
  • Contesting — ARDF as a competitive sport

docs.title: Contesting
docs.text:
Ham radio contesting is the "competitive sport" of the hobby — organised events where the goal is not a lengthy conversation, but rather making as many contacts as possible within a set time frame. Contests are an excellent way to stress-test your station and operating skills, meet operators from around the world, and earn awards and placements.

If you've ever worked QSO after QSO on 20 m during a great opening, you're already close to contesting — all that's missing is a logger and a bit of strategy.

docs.title: What Is a Contest QSO?
docs.text:
Unlike a regular ragchew where you exchange names, locations, and equipment details, a contest exchange take665 ghhhhhhhhhhhfggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggs only a few seconds. Every contest has a prescribed exchange format: typically a signal report (59 or 599) plus one additional element that scores points — CQ zone, ITU zone, serial number, grid locator, and so on.

Speed and accuracy matter more than the quality of conversation.

docs.title: Example SSB Exchange (CQ WW DX)
docs.text:
9A1ZG: "CQ Contest, CQ Contest, Niner Alpha One Zulu Golf, 9A1ZG"
DJ3AB: "DJ3AB"
9A1ZG: "DJ3AB, 59, 15"          ← signal report 59, CQ zone 15 for Croatia
DJ3AB: "59, 14"                  ← signal report 59, CQ zone 14 for Germany
9A1ZG: "73, QRZ? 9A1ZG"

The entire exchange takes 10–20 seconds. Experienced contesters aim for under 10.

docs.title: Example CW Exchange
docs.text:
9A1ZG: CQ TEST 9A1ZG 9A1ZG
DJ3AB: DJ3AB
9A1ZG: DJ3AB 599 001     ← RST 599, our serial number: 001
DJ3AB: 599 088           ← RST 599, his serial number: 088
9A1ZG: TU 9A1ZG

Why always 59 or 599?

In contests, nearly everyone sends a report of 59 or 599 regardless of actual signal quality. This is a convention — the goal is to exchange the required data as quickly as possible, not to evaluate the signal. Exceptions exist in contests that score based on real reports (e.g., the Worked All Germany contest).

docs.title: Operator Categories
docs.text:
To keep contests fair, participants submit their log in one of several defined categories, based on the number of operators, transmitter power, and use of the DX cluster (a spotting network that alerts you to active stations):

Category Description Max TX DX Cluster? Typical use Single Op (SO) One operator doing everything — frequency management, logging, calling CQ 1 No Beginners and solo experts alike Single Op Assisted (SOA) One operator, but with access to the DX cluster for spots 1 Yes Great for hunting rare entities Multi Single (M/S) Multiple operators, but only one transmitter active at any given time 1 Yes Club teams sharing operating duties Multi Two (M/2) Multiple operators, two transmitters running simultaneously on different bands 2 Yes Organised club stations Multi Multi (M/M) Multiple operators, one transmitter per band running simultaneously Unlimited Yes Large DX expeditions and mega stations QRP Maximum output power of 5 W 1 Varies Enthusiasts of minimalist stations

In addition to these main categories, many contests offer overlay categories — supplemental divisions you compete in simultaneously:

  • Rookie — licensed for fewer than 3 years
  • Classic — no assistance and no remote operation
  • Youth — operators under 25 years of age

Choose your category carefully

When you submit your log, you declare a category. Entering Low Power when you ran 100 W is grounds for disqualification. Operators in the same category compete against each other — getting the category right puts you on a level playing field.

docs.title: Scoring — Why Multipliers Matter
docs.text:
In almost every contest, your final score is not just a sum of contacts but the product of QSO points and multipliers:

\[\text{Score} = \sum \text{QSO Points} \times \sum \text{Multipliers}\]

What are multipliers? Special targets you can claim once per band (or once per contest) — for example, each CQ zone, each country (DXCC entity), each US state, each grid square... A single new multiplier can be worth more than a dozen "ordinary" QSOs.

docs.title: Scoring in CQ WW DX (the world's largest contest)
docs.text:
Element Scoring rule QSO with your own country (9A ↔ 9A) 0 points QSO within the same continent (EU ↔ EU) 1 point QSO across continents (HR ↔ USA, JA, VK...) 3 points Multipliers — CQ zones Each new zone, once per band Multipliers — DXCC entities Each new country, once per band

Score calculation example

An operator makes 800 QSOs in 24 hours — 300 within Europe (1 point each) and 500 across continents to North America, Asia, and Oceania (3 points each).

  • QSO points: 300 × 1 + 500 × 3 = 1,800 points
  • Multipliers: 38 CQ zones + 120 DXCC countries = 158 multipliers
  • Total score: 1,800 × 158 = 284,400 points

This is why working a rare station from zone 26 or a tiny island DXCC entity is far more valuable than ten contacts with common European stations.

docs.title: Major Contest Calendar
docs.text:
Contests run throughout the year. Here are the most significant events you'll encounter:

Contest Mode Typical Date Exchange Rules CQ WW DX SSB SSB Last weekend of October RS + CQ zone cqww.com CQ WW DX CW CW Last weekend of November RST + CQ zone cqww.com CQ WPX SSB SSB Last weekend of March RS + serial number cqwpx.com CQ WPX CW CW Last weekend of May RST + serial number cqwpx.com ARRL DX SSB SSB 3rd weekend of February RS + US state or power arrl.org ARRL DX CW CW 3rd weekend of March RST + US state or power arrl.org WAE DX CW CW 2nd weekend of August RST + QTC blocks darc.de WAE DX SSB SSB 2nd weekend of September RS + QTC blocks darc.de IOTA Contest SSB/CW Last full weekend of July RS(T) + serial or IOTA ref. rsgbcc.org 9A DX Contest SSB/CW 3rd weekend of December RS(T) + county (9A) / ITU zone (DX) hamradio.hr

WARC bands — no contesting allowed

The 30 m (10 MHz), 17 m (18 MHz), and 12 m (24 MHz) bands are WARC bands. By international agreement (IARU), organising or participating in contests on these bands is prohibited. They serve as "quiet zones" for normal QSOs and propagation research. See Band Plans for details.

docs.title: Contest Logging Software
docs.text:
In a contest you don't log manually — you use specialised software that integrates with your radio (CAT control), checks duplicates automatically, displays multipliers in real time, and generates a Cabrillo log for submission.

N1MM+ LoggerDXLog.netWin-Test

The most popular free contest logger for Windows. Used by everyone from first-time contesters to world champions.

  • Strengths: Supports 1,000+ different contests, excellent CAT integration with virtually any radio, voice keyer for automated SSB calls, a large active community with detailed documentation.
  • The built-in rate meter shows your contacts per hour in real time.
  • Download: n1mmwp.hamdocs.com
  • Price: Free

A modern, fast, and highly capable logger, especially favoured by Multi-Op stations. Developed partly by Croatian contesters.

  • Strengths: Excellent networked logging for M/S and M/M categories, real-time LAN synchronisation within the station, cloud backup of logs.
  • Supports all major contests with a clean modern UI.
  • Download: dxlog.net
  • Price: Free for amateur use

A classic that serious contesters and large stations rely on. Based on the keyboard shortcuts of the legendary CT logger from the 1980s.

  • Strengths: Extremely stable, decades of proven use, supported at all serious Multi-Multi stations worldwide.
  • Download: win-test.com
  • Price: Paid shareware (~€50)

Start with N1MM+

Install N1MM+, run through the setup wizard (callsign, CAT port), and try it a week before the contest — not on contest day. Every major contest has a preconfigured template in N1MM+ and you can be logging within minutes.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text: Contesting is not just about antenna gain — strategy plays a huge role.

docs.title: Running vs. Search & Pounce (S&P)
docs.text:
Running means holding your own clear frequency and calling CQ TEST continuously. Stations find you and respond. This is productive when you have a strong signal and a free spot on the band, but it requires a solid antenna and favourable propagation.

Search & Pounce (S&P) means tuning across the band, finding stations calling CQ, and answering them. This is ideal for beginners because you don't need to "hold" a frequency, and you still accumulate QSOs and multipliers efficiently.

Practical approach: Start S&P to collect multipliers from rare entities, then — if you find a quiet gap on the band — try a short CQ run and see who responds.

docs.title: Rate vs. Multipliers
docs.text:
Operators sustaining 200 QSOs/hour almost always prefer to keep running rather than interrupt the flow for a single multiplier. But the maths shifts when rate is lower: one unique multiplier can be worth more than 10–20 ordinary QSOs.

Simple rule of thumb: When your rate drops below 50 QSOs/hour, it's worth pausing to hunt for new multipliers across the band.

docs.title: Band Hopping and Propagation
docs.text:
Propagation changes throughout the day and night. 20 m is open to North America in the UTC afternoon; 40 m and 80 m perform best for DX at night; 15 m and 10 m only shine when solar flux (SFI) is elevated above ~120. For a deeper dive, see Radio Wave Propagation.

The grey line — a contester's golden hour

The twilight zone (grey line) moving across the Earth at sunrise and sunset creates brief but exceptional propagation windows. Experienced contesters plan their most intensive activity around these intervals to capture multipliers from the most distant parts of the world.

docs.title: 9A Contests (Croatia)
docs.text:
The Croatian Amateur Radio Association (HRS) organises and supports several traditional contests:

The 9A DX Contest is the main Croatian contest, open to all stations worldwide. It runs on the third weekend of December, from Saturday 14:00 UTC to Sunday 13:59 UTC, across the 160–10 m bands in mixed mode (SSB and CW).

  • Exchange for 9A stations: RS(T) + two-letter county abbreviation (ZG = Zagreb, ST = Split, RI = Rijeka, OS = Osijek, etc.)
  • Exchange for DX stations (outside Croatia): RS(T) + ITU zone
  • Scoring: Contacts with 9A stations carry additional multiplier value
  • Submit your Cabrillo log to hamradio.hr within 7 days of the contest closing.

9A Activity Contests are regular VHF/UHF events encouraging on-air activity on 144 MHz, 432 MHz, and 1,296 MHz. They use IARU grid locators for exchanges and are great for testing antennas and getting a feel for the contest format at lower stakes.

For the current calendar of Croatian and HRS-organised contests, visit www.hamradio.hr.

docs.title: Submitting Your Log — Cabrillo Format
docs.text:
After the contest, you submit your log to the organiser in the standardised Cabrillo format — a plain-text file with a header and a list of every QSO. All contest loggers generate Cabrillo with a single click.

In N1MM+: File → Generate Cabrillo Log

Example Cabrillo file

START-OF-LOG: 3.0
CALLSIGN: 9A1ZG
CONTEST: CQ-WW-SSB
CATEGORY-OPERATOR: SINGLE-OP
CATEGORY-ASSISTED: NON-ASSISTED
CATEGORY-BAND: ALL
CATEGORY-POWER: LOW
CLAIMED-SCORE: 284400
OPERATORS: 9A1ZG
NAME: Jasmin Surname
ADDRESS: Zagreb, Croatia
CREATED-BY: N1MM+ 1.0.10234.0
...
QSO: 14195 PH 2024-10-26 1201 9A1ZG         59 15   DJ3AB         59 14
QSO: 14195 PH 2024-10-26 1203 9A1ZG         59 15   W1ABC         59 05
END-OF-LOG:

Log submission deadlines are typically 5–7 days after the contest ends. Even if you only have 20 QSOs — submit your log! You receive a certificate of participation and your result appears in the official statistics, which is valuable data for the organisers too.

docs.title: Getting Started — A Guide for First-Time Contesters
docs.text:
Contesting can look intimidating from the outside, but the entry point is simple:

  1. Install N1MM+ — it's free. Launch it, enter your callsign, and configure your CAT connection to the radio. Do this a few days before the contest, not on the day.
  2. Choose a lower-pressure contest to start — the 9A Activity Contest (VHF) or the IOTA Contest are good first choices. CQ WW is excellent but the sheer volume of stations can be overwhelming for a first outing.
  3. Enter Single Op Low Power — no pressure, and you'll be comparing yourself only with operators in the same class.
  4. Work S&P only at first — tune the VFO, find stations calling CQ TEST, and answer them. You don't need to hold a frequency or worry about running.
  5. Participate for 2–4 hours, not 48 — get a feel for the rhythm, the logging, and the S&P flow. A full weekend contest is a challenge for next time.
  6. Always submit your log — even with 15 QSOs. N1MM+ generates Cabrillo in seconds, and every submitted log counts.

You don't need a big station

Excellent contesters regularly achieve strong results with a dipole and 100 W. Strategy, propagation knowledge, and operating skill outweigh raw power. Start with what you have.

docs.title: DXing — Long-Distance Operating
docs.text:
DXing is one of the most exciting disciplines in amateur radio — hunting distant and rare stations (DX stations) from around the world. "DX" comes from the telegraph abbreviation for "distance", and operators who pursue it are called DXers.

The combination of propagation knowledge, strategy, patience, and operating skill makes DXing a pursuit that never loses its appeal — there is always a new country, a new island group, a new expedition to "catch".

docs.title: What Is DX?
docs.text:
The definition depends on the band:

  • On HF (shortwave), DX typically means a contact with another continent — the Americas, Africa, Asia, or Oceania from Europe.
  • On VHF/UHF (2 m, 70 cm), even a 500–1,000 km contact via Sporadic-E propagation qualifies as DX.
  • Microwave (SHF) bands have their own DX horizons — sometimes only a few hundred kilometres due to atmospheric absorption.

Why is DXing so popular?

There are approximately 340 DXCC entities on the ARRL list. The entry-level DXCC award requires confirmed contacts with 100 different entities. "Mixed DXCC" with all 340 entities confirmed is achieved only by the most dedicated operators — and within the community it carries legendary status.

docs.title: DXCC Entities — What They Are and How They're Counted
docs.text:
DXCC (DX Century Club) is the ARRL (American Radio Relay League) programme certifying contacts with distinct geographic entities. DXCC entities are not always sovereign states — the list uses its own rules based on geographic separation, political status, and distance. That is why it includes:

Example Reason for separate entity Hawaii (KH6) Geographically remote, separate callsign prefix Alaska (KL7) Geographically separate from the continental USA Palagruža (9A/island group) Rare, small, remote from Croatian mainland Antarctica Special political status, extreme inaccessibility Ceuta and Melilla (EA9) Politically separate from mainland Spain

Of the ~340 entities, around 40–50 are considered "rare DX" — their activations draw thousands of hunters worldwide.

docs.title: How to Work DX — Core Technique
1. Listening Is Everything
docs.text:
90% of DXing is listening. An experienced DX operator recognises a rare station by its signal, prefix, and operating style, and calls at the right moment. A beginner who immediately starts calling without hearing the exchange wastes precious time and annoys others.

Listening routine: - Tune across the bands looking for unusual callsign prefixes - Monitor the DX cluster for fresh spots - Recognise the characteristic pile-up sound (a mass of stations calling in broken, overlapping rhythm)

docs.title: 2. The DX Cluster — The DX Community's Central Radar
docs.text:
The DX cluster is a network of servers where operators publish ("spot") active DX stations they have heard. A spot contains the frequency, DX callsign, mode, UTC time, and a comment.

Popular resources: - DX Summit — the most widely used cluster web interface - DXMaps — visual map of spots in real time - DX Watch — alternative filtered display - HamAlert (mobile app) — push notifications for specific prefixes or bands

Set filters on the cluster

Unfiltered cluster displays are overwhelming. Filter by band (20 m, 40 m…) and region ("Europe only" or "missing DXCC only"), and you will immediately see only what is relevant to your operating session.

docs.title: 3. The Pile-Up — When Everyone Calls at Once
docs.text:
When a rare DX station is active — especially an expedition to an uninhabited island — hundreds or thousands of operators call simultaneously. This is called a pile-up.

How to behave in a pile-up:

  • Transmit only your own callsign — once, clearly. Do not repeat it three times, do not add "CQ", "QRZ", or "please".
  • Listen to how the DX station responds — pick up the rhythm and the fragments of callsigns it is calling back.
  • Try briefly between others' calls, not while someone else is transmitting.
  • Be patient — it sometimes takes 15–30 minutes of active monitoring before you break through.

Split operation — the most important rule

Almost all rare DX stations work split — they transmit on one frequency (e.g. 14.195 MHz) and listen on another (e.g. 14.200–14.210 MHz, "UP 5 to 10").

Never call on the DX station's transmit frequency! You will interfere with the DX station and anger the entire community. Always listen for where the DX says it is listening, then call there.

docs.title: Tools and Resources for DXers
docs.text: Tool Purpose Link DX Summit DX cluster web interface dxsummit.fi DXWatch Alternative cluster view dx.watch DXMaps Visual propagation and spot map dxmaps.com Club Log Contact log, OQRS for QSL clublog.org LoTW ARRL Logbook of the World, DXCC confirmations arrl.org/lotw DXCC Listings Current entity list arrl.org/dxcc DX Code of Conduct Ethical code of the DX community dx-code.org PSKReporter Real-time FT8/FT4 propagation map pskreporter.info HamAlert Push notifications for DX spots hamalert.org

docs.title: QSL Cards and Awards
docs.text:
Making the contact is only the first step. DXCC and other awards require QSL confirmations:

  • Paper QSL card — the traditional route, sent by post or through the QSL bureau
  • LoTW (Logbook of the World) — digital confirmation, the most widely accepted for DXCC. Requires an ARRL digital certificate.
  • eQSL — alternative digital confirmation, accepted by fewer awards
  • Club Log OQRS — direct QSL card from DX expeditions, paid via PayPal, fast and reliable

QSL bureau — the economical route for paper cards

Posting individual cards to the USA or Japan is expensive. The national QSL bureau (your local IARU member society) collects cards from your country's operators and forwards them in bulk to international bureaus, which then distribute them locally. Slow (6–12 months), but nearly free.

docs.title: DX Expeditions
docs.text:
Some DXCC entities are so remote or inaccessible that they are only activated by specially organised DX expeditions (DXpeditions). Teams of 5–20 operators travel to an uninhabited island or restricted territory and operate for several weeks with multiple stations, 24 hours a day.

Notable expeditions: - Bouvet Island (3Y0) — one of the most wanted entities in the world, a sub-Antarctic island in the Atlantic - Clipperton Island (FO/C) — French Pacific atoll, each expedition draws 100,000+ QSOs - Heard Island (VK0H) — sub-Antarctic Australian island, extremely rare - Palagruža (9A/island group) — our Croatian rarity, a small rocky island in the open Adriatic

Follow DXpedition announcements at DX World and The Daily DX.

docs.title: DXing on Digital Modes
docs.text:
FT8 and FT4 have revolutionised DXing — weak signals that could never be heard before now get through. Many DXers who never succeeded with SSB are now working new entities with FT8.

At the same time, this has created a new dynamic: pile-ups on FT8 are different (automated sequence), and some DX expeditions operate exclusively on FT8, which is controversial among "purists".

For setting up an FT8 station, see FT8 — Practical Guide.

docs.title: The DXCC Award — How to Start
docs.text:
1. Register for LoTW — immediately. Without LoTW you will need paper QSL cards to document every entity.
  2. Club Log — use a logger that uploads to Club Log (Log4OM, N1MM+, DXKeeper). Club Log shows you which entities you are still missing.
  3. Monitor DX Summit — filter for "missing DXCC" and your preferred band.
  4. Start with accessible entities — Europe alone has over a hundred entities that are regularly active. When you reach 100, apply for DXCC Mixed.
  5. Progress toward the rare ones — with experience, better antennas, and propagation knowledge, tackle the more challenging entities.

100 entities is achievable by anyone

A dipole on 20 m and a standard 100 W transceiver are sufficient for the first 100 DXCC entities. The rest requires more patience, propagation awareness, and — sometimes — better antennas.

docs.title: Portable Operations — SOTA, POTA and IOTA
docs.text:
Operating radio in the field is one of the most rewarding combined experiences in the hobby — a mountain summit, morning silence, and a QSO with Japan through a dipole made from wire and a fishing pole. Portable radio activism integrates nature, technology, and operating skills into a unique experience that no other activity quite matches.

The three most organised portable activities are SOTA (mountain summits), POTA (parks and protected areas) and IOTA (islands). Each has its own scoring system, awards, and community of enthusiasts.

docs.title: SOTA — Summits On The Air
docs.text: SOTA is an international programme encouraging amateur radio operators to make contacts from registered mountain summits. The programme is coordinated by the Sota Mapping Project and organised through national associations (for Croatia: 9A SOTA).

docs.title: Activator and Chaser
docs.text:
Two roles exist in the SOTA programme:

  • Activator — the operator who climbs the summit and makes contacts from there. Must be physically on the summit or within the "activation zone" (25 m vertically below the summit). Carries all equipment.
  • Chaser — an operator who contacts activators from their shack or any other location.

For each successful activation, the activator must make a minimum of 4 QSOs. Duplicates with the same station do not count.

docs.title: Scoring and Awards
docs.text:
Each SOTA summit carries a point value from 1 to 10, based on height and relative elevation gain:

Award (Activator) Points Mountain Goat 1,000 points Super Goat 5,000 points Ultra Goat 10,000 points Award (Chaser) Contacts Shack Sloth 1,000 QSOs with SOTA summits

Winter bonus: Activations between 1 November and 31 March earn double points for summits above 900 m. It pays to go out in winter!

Summit-to-Summit (S2S): A contact between two simultaneously active SOTA summits earns a special bonus and is a memorable experience — coordinate with another activator and call each other from two peaks.

docs.title: Croatia in SOTA
docs.text:
The 9A SOTA association covers all Croatian mountains. Popular targets:

  • Sljeme (9A/CA-001) — Medvednica, easily accessible, perfect for beginners
  • Obruč (9A/PR-005) — Gorski Kotar, excellent propagation westward
  • Velika Bielolasica (9A/PR-001) — highest peak in Gorski Kotar, 1,534 m
  • Sveti Jure (9A/DA-001) — Biokovo, 1,762 m, view over the Adriatic

Full list of Croatian summits: SOTA database — 9A

docs.title: Alerts and Spots — Why They Matter
docs.text:
An activator who announces the activation in advance and self-spots from the summit will attract far more chasers:

  • Alert — advance notice > 24 hours on SOTAwatch3. Chasers monitor alerts and will be waiting for you.
  • Spot — a public announcement saying "I am active now on X frequency". Self-spot via SMS, mobile app (SOTA Spotter, Reflector), or ask a chaser to spot you.

No spot — no chasers

An unannounced activation is possible, but you will be searching for QSOs in the dark. With a spot, chasers are already waiting and you can have 4 contacts within minutes. Always spot!

docs.title: POTA — Parks On The Air
docs.text: POTA is an international programme encouraging activations from national parks, nature parks, protected areas, and natural reserves. Less physically demanding than SOTA — you can often park your car 10 metres from the antenna.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Operate from a registered park (database at pota.app)
  • Minimum 10 QSOs for one activation
  • The same park can be activated multiple times (each counts separately)
  • No time limit — even 30 minutes of operating from a park is valid

docs.title: Croatia and POTA
docs.text:
The POTA database contains over 30 registered Croatian locations:

  • NP Plitvice Lakes (HR-0002)
  • NP Krka (HR-0004)
  • NP Paklenica (HR-0003)
  • PP Medvednica (HR-0010)
  • PP Vransko Lake (HR-0018)
  • ...and many more

Full list of Croatian parks: POTA — Croatia

POTA and repeated activations

Unlike SOTA where a summit counts once a year per activator, the same POTA park can be activated every week. Many operators combine a walk in their local park with an hour of radio — an excellent combination.

docs.title: IOTA — Islands On The Air
docs.text: IOTA is the oldest of the three programmes, founded in 1964 in the UK (RSGB). The goal is to make contacts with islands — each island group has its own IOTA reference number (e.g. EU-016 for the Kvarner islands).

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
• Chaser — collects IOTA references by working active island stations. The award starts at 100 confirmed IOTA references.
  • Activator — operates from an island and provides confirmations to chasers. Activation requires a minimum of 100 QSOs.

docs.title: Croatia and IOTA
docs.text:
The Adriatic is a wealth of IOTA references:

IOTA Reference Island group Notes EU-016 Kvarner islands (Krk, Cres, Lošinj, Rab…) Regularly activated EU-110 North Dalmatian islands (Zadar archipelago) Active EU-136 South Dalmatian islands (Šibenik, Trogir, Split area) Active EU-016 Korčula, Hvar, Brač Active EU-090 Palagruža Extremely rare! Every activation draws a pile-up

Palagruža (EU-090) is a small rocky island in the middle of the Adriatic, 68 km from Korčula. Inaccessible, rare, sought-after — every activation becomes a European-scale DX event.

docs.title: Transceivers
docs.text: Radio Power Bands Weight Price (approx.) Notes Yaesu FT-818ND 6 W HF + VHF + UHF 830 g ~€500 Long-standing classic, all-in-one Elecraft KX2 10 W HF 340 g ~€850 Outstanding QRP transceiver, premium Icom IC-705 10 W HF + VHF + UHF 990 g ~€1,200 SDR architecture, Bluetooth, Scope TR-USDX 5–10 W HF (5 bands) 170 g €80–150 Ultra-cheap DIY kit QCX-mini 5 W One band, CW 130 g ~€60 Kit, CW only, excellent for SOTA

docs.title: Antennas
docs.text: Antenna Weight Setup time Bands Notes End-Fed Half Wave (EFHW) ~200 g 5–10 min One (more with tuner) Most versatile, throw wire into tree Linked dipole ~300 g 10–15 min Multiple (change in field) Swap band by adding/removing segment Fishing rod vertical ~400 g 8–12 min 20–10 m (with tuner) No counterpoise needed, tall Moxon ~250 g 15 min One band 2-element directional characteristic

docs.title: Power
docs.text:
Battery Capacity Weight Runtime (5 W TX/RX) Notes LiFePO4 3 Ah 3 Ah / 12 V ~250 g ~3 hours Short SOTA activations LiFePO4 5 Ah 5 Ah / 12 V ~380 g ~5 hours Standard choice LiFePO4 10 Ah 10 Ah / 12 V ~840 g ~10 hours Longer POTA sessions Solar panel 10 W — ~700 g Unlimited (with sun) Excellent for summer POTA

LiFePO4 is the portable standard

Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries are superior to everything else for portable use: lightweight, safe (no swelling or fire like Li-Ion), long cycle life (2,000+), and stable voltage. Buy once, use for years.

docs.title: Activation Checklist
docs.text:
The day before: - [ ] Check the weather forecast (especially for SOTA — lightning on a summit is deadly!) - [ ] Post a SOTA alert at sotawatch.sota.org.uk - [ ] Charge all batteries fully - [ ] Test the antenna and cables at home

Day of activation: - [ ] Radio, battery, antenna, cables - [ ] Phone with mobile data (for spotting) - [ ] Log notebook and pen (or app) - [ ] Food, water, appropriate clothing - [ ] For SOTA: terrain map, compass (or GPS), first aid kit

On location: - [ ] Set up antenna and check SWR - [ ] Post your spot immediately after setup - [ ] Log each QSO (date, UTC, callsign, frequency, mode, RST) - [ ] For SOTA: minimum 4 QSOs

Lightning on SOTA summits

At the first signs of thunder or dark clouds — immediately remove the antenna and descend. A metal antenna on an exposed mountain summit is a catastophic combination. No QSO is worth more than safety. Mountain weather can arrive in minutes without warning.

docs.title: Satellite Communications
docs.text:
Amateur radio operators have their own satellites in orbit — and you can use them with a handheld radio and a homemade antenna. Satellite communications ("sat") is one of the most technically fascinating branches of the hobby, combining orbital mechanics, Doppler physics, and RF engineering.

There are currently more than 20 active amateur satellites in various configurations — from simple FM repeaters accessible with a handheld, to advanced linear transponders for SSB and CW contacts.

docs.title: How Do Amateur Satellites Work?
docs.text:
Most amateur satellites are "flying repeaters" — they receive a signal from Earth (uplink), amplify it, and retransmit it back (downlink) on a different frequency.

Term Explanation Uplink The frequency on which you transmit your signal toward the satellite Downlink The frequency on which you receive the satellite (it transmits toward Earth) Mode The uplink/downlink band combination (e.g. Mode V/U = VHF up, UHF down) Transponder A linear "repeater" that passes an entire frequency segment — not just FM, but SSB and CW too Footprint The geographic area from which the satellite is accessible (visible) at any given moment

docs.title: Satellite Types
docs.text:
FM satellites (easier to use): - Work like a standard FM repeater, but orbiting in space - A handheld transceiver (5 W) and a simple Yagi held in your hand are sufficient - Examples: SO-50, AO-91, AO-92

Linear transponder satellites (more advanced): - Pass an entire frequency band — SSB, CW, and digital modes simultaneously in a "window" - Allow multiple simultaneous contacts (not simplex like FM) - Require better equipment and tracking antennas (azimuth/elevation rotators) - Examples: AO-7, FO-29, CAS-4A/B, XW-2 series

docs.title: Doppler Correction — The Key Challenge
docs.text:
Amateur satellites travel at ~7.8 km/s (LEO — low Earth orbit). At that speed, the Doppler effect (the same shift you hear when an emergency vehicle passes you — the siren changes pitch) causes a measurable frequency shift:

  • At 145 MHz: Doppler shift can be up to ±3.5 kHz
  • At 435 MHz: Shift can be up to ±10 kHz

In practice: At the start of a pass, the satellite is approaching — frequency appears higher (you need to listen slightly above the nominal downlink). At the end of the pass, it recedes — frequency drops. You correct the frequency on your receiver manually or automatically throughout the pass.

Software such as Gpredict does this automatically in combination with CAT control of the radio.

\[\Delta f = \frac{v}{c} \times f_0\]

where \(v\) is the radial velocity of the satellite toward/away from you, \(c\) is the speed of light, and \(f_0\) is the nominal frequency.

For FM satellites — manual correction is fine

On FM satellites, the Doppler shift is not fatal — if you are 2 kHz off nominal, the FM squelch will still open. Manual correction every 30–60 seconds is perfectly sufficient.

docs.title: When and Where Is the Satellite Visible?
docs.text: Amateur satellites in low Earth orbit (LEO) pass over your location several times a day, but each pass lasts only 8–15 minutes. You need to know precisely when and in which direction.

docs.title: Tracking Tools
docs.text:
Tool Platform Description Gpredict Win/Linux/Mac Desktop tracker, CAT integration for Doppler, free ISS Detector Android/iOS Simple mobile tracker focused on ISS Heavens-Above Web Visual pass display for your location N2YO Web Real-time orbital display with map SatPC32 Windows Classic tracker with CAT + rotator control

All tools require up-to-date Keplerian elements (TLE — Two-Line Elements) describing the satellite's orbit. These are downloaded automatically from NASA/AMSAT databases.

Update TLE two hours before the pass

Keplerian elements become outdated. Update them at least once a day, especially for lower-orbit satellites (ISS ~400 km, SO-50 ~600 km).

docs.title: Getting Started: FM Satellites
docs.text:
Your first satellite contact on an FM satellite requires surprisingly little:

Minimum: - Dual-band handheld (VHF + UHF) — e.g. Baofeng UV-5R, Yaesu FT-70D, Kenwood TH-D75 - Improvised cross-band Yagi for 2 m + 70 cm (can be built from aluminium rod for a few euros) - Mobile phone with Gpredict or ISS Detector (to track the pass)

Operating technique: 1. Find out when the satellite passes over your location and the azimuth of the pass 2. Set the uplink frequency (with Doppler correction +3 kHz at the start of the pass) 3. Listen on the downlink and check whether you hear the satellite (characteristic repeater noise) 4. Call briefly: "9A1ZG, any station via SO-50?" 5. Follow the pass and manually correct Doppler as the satellite moves

Common FM satellite frequencies

SO-50: Uplink 145.850 MHz (PL tone 67.0 Hz), Downlink 436.795 MHz

AO-91 (Fox-1B): Uplink 435.250 MHz, Downlink 145.960 MHz

ISS: Uplink/Downlink 145.825 MHz (APRS) or 437.800 MHz (voice repeater, when active)

docs.title: ISS — International Space Station
docs.text:
ISS is the most famous "amateur satellite" — the onboard ARS (Amateur Radio Station) operates under callsigns NA1SS (USA) and RS0ISS (Russia).

Amateur radio activities on ISS:

  • APRS gateway (145.825 MHz) — ISS automatically digipeats APRS packets from Earth. You can bounce APRS packets through ISS!
  • Voice QSO — when crew are available and the antenna is active, they operate on 145.800 MHz or announced frequencies
  • SSTV events — ISS periodically transmits SSTV (Slow Scan TV) images that you can decode with standard software (MMSSTV)
  • ARISS school projects — the ARISS (Amateur Radio on the International Space Station) programme organises contacts between ISS crew and schools worldwide

ARISS and schools

ARISS (ariss.org) facilitates direct contacts between astronauts aboard ISS and students in schools. Amateur radio astronauts include Thomas Pesquet (F4KLP), Sunita Williams (KC5ZTH), and many others.

docs.title: Advanced Satellites — Linear Transponders
docs.text:
For SSB and CW contacts via satellite you need:

  • A more capable transceiver with fine frequency tuning (< 10 Hz steps)
  • Crossed-Yagi antenna (circularly polarised) for azimuth + elevation tracking
  • Rotator system — the antenna must follow the satellite across the sky
  • Gpredict + CAT — automated Doppler correction

Popular linear satellites: - FO-29 (JAS-2) — Japanese satellite, V/U linear transponder - AO-7 — launched in 1974 and still partially operational! - CAS-4A and CAS-4B — Chinese satellites with excellent transponders, active from 2022 onwards - XW-2 series — several Chinese linear transponder satellites

docs.title: EME — Earth-Moon-Earth (Moonbounce)
docs.text:
EME is the extreme form of satellite communication — the signal travels to the Moon and back, totalling ~760,000 km. The path loss is approximately 250–270 dB, requiring extremely sensitive receivers and high-gain antennas.

  • Popular on 2 m (144 MHz) and 70 cm (432 MHz)
  • Uses JT65 and Q65 digital protocols (from the WSJT-X package)
  • Minimum practical equipment: 4-element Yagi array, 100+ W, low-noise LNA preamplifier
  • Experimentation began in the 1950s; today accessible to dedicated enthusiasts

docs.text:

  • Antennas and Feed Lines — Yagi and circularly polarised antennas
