  • Radio Wave Propagation — difference between terrestrial and satellite propagation

docs.title: CB Radio (Citizens Band)
docs.text:
Citizens Band (CB) radio is a publicly available radio service on frequencies around 27 MHz that requires no exam or licence to use — buy a radio, switch it on, and you can communicate. Despite this, there are technical regulations and unwritten rules of courtesy that users are expected to follow.

CB is popular among truck drivers, off-road enthusiasts, hikers, preppers, and anyone who needs reliable communication over short to medium distances without bureaucratic barriers.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Introduction to CB — history, purpose, who uses CB today and why
  • Regulations and Channels — complete 40-channel table, EU/Croatia regulations, power limits and modulations
  • CB Equipment — mobile and base stations, antennas, SWR tuning, model overview

docs.title: CB at a Glance
docs.text: Parameter Value Frequency range 26.965 – 27.405 MHz Number of channels 40 Modulations AM, FM, SSB (LSB/USB) Max. power (AM/FM) 4 W Max. power (SSB) 12 W PEP Licence/exam Not required Typical range (mobile) 3–15 km Typical range (base) 15–50 km, DX hundreds of km Emergency channel Channel 9 (27.065 MHz) Road/truckers channel Channel 19 (27.185 MHz)

docs.title: CB vs. Amateur Radio — Key Differences
docs.text:
CB and amateur radio share the same RF technology, but they are different services with different rules:

Aspect CB Radio Amateur Radio Exam / licence ❌ Not required ✅ Technical exam, licence Max. power 4 W (AM/FM), 12 W (SSB) Up to 1,500 W (depending on licence class) Frequency freedom ❌ Only 40 fixed channels ✅ Entire bands (HF, VHF, UHF…) Modes AM, FM, SSB SSB, CW, FM, FT8, SSTV, and many more Antenna Regulated max. gain/height Essentially unrestricted DX capability Occasional, propagation-dependent Regular, worldwide Infrastructure None Repeaters, satellites, ISS Entry cost Low (€50–200) Medium (€200–1,000+)

CB as a stepping stone

Many experienced amateur radio operators began on CB. They learned the basics of propagation, antennas, and radio etiquette, then passed the exam and moved on. If you are a CB user considering amateur radio — you are already well positioned!

docs.title: Propagation on 27 MHz
docs.text:
Although CB looks like a simple service, propagation at 27 MHz (11 m wavelength) can be surprisingly interesting:

  • Normal ranges: A mobile station with 4 W achieves 5–15 km on flat terrain or in cities
  • Tropospheric propagation: With favourable atmospheric conditions, ranges extend to 50–200 km
  • Sporadic-E (Es): In spring and summer, the ionospheric E layer can reflect CB signals enabling contacts over 500–2,000 km — this is CB DX

The solar cycle and CB DX

Like the amateur 10 m band (28 MHz), CB is subject to solar influence. During solar maximum, F2 propagation can bring DX contacts of 5,000+ km on 27 MHz. Experienced CB DXers monitor solar forecasts just like amateur radio operators!

docs.title: Section Links
docs.text:
• Introduction to CB
  • Regulations and Channels
  • CB Equipment
  • Amateur Radio Regulations — comparison with the amateur system

docs.title: CB Regulations and Channels
docs.text: CB radio in Europe and Croatia uses 40 channels in the 26.965–27.405 MHz frequency band. Each channel has a fixed frequency — unlike amateur radio where operators can tune freely across a band, CB users follow a prescribed channel plan.

docs.title: Permitted Technical Parameters (Croatia / EU)
docs.text:
Parameter Value Frequency range 26.960 – 27.410 MHz Number of channels 40 (standard European plan) Channel spacing 10 kHz AM modulation Permitted, max. 4 W carrier (ERP) FM modulation Permitted, max. 4 W (ERP) SSB modulation Permitted, max. 12 W PEP Antenna gain Not separately restricted; device must be type-approved Standard EN 300 433 (EU) Regulatory body (HR) HAKOM Licence Not required (general authorisation)

AM vs FM vs SSB — which modulation?

  • AM (Amplitude Modulation) is the original CB modulation — cuts through well, compatible with all radios, but less efficient (55% of power goes into the carrier which carries no information)
  • FM (Frequency Modulation) is cleaner, less susceptible to noise and interference — preferred for local work
  • SSB (Single Sideband) is the most efficient — all power goes into the speech signal, giving 2–3× the range. Essential for DX work. LSB (Lower Sideband) is the CB SSB convention.

docs.title: Channel 9 — Emergency Channel 🚨
docs.text:
Channel 9 (27.065 MHz) is the internationally recognised emergency channel. Reserved exclusively for: - Calling for help in emergencies (accidents, missing persons, vehicle breakdowns) - Communication with CB-equipped emergency services - Coordination during disasters

Channel 9 is not for conversation

Using Channel 9 for normal chat or "testing equipment" is prohibited and irresponsible — someone in need of help may be unable to reach the frequency.

docs.title: Channel 19 — Road 🚛
docs.text:
Channel 19 (27.185 MHz) is the European standard for driver communication on motorways and main roads. Drivers exchange: - Traffic conditions and accident reports - Speed camera warnings - Calling and switching to working channel

Standard practice: call on Ch19, switch to a free channel for the conversation.

docs.title: Channels 38–40 — SSB DX
docs.text: Channels 38, 39 and 40 are the conventional SSB DX channels. LSB is the standard for CB SSB communication. On these channels you can hear contacts with operators from across Europe.

docs.title: Alpha Channels
docs.text: "Alpha" (or "A") channels are intermediate frequencies included in Australian and American CB channel plans (e.g. 27.045 MHz between channels 7 and 8). The European standard plan does not include these as standard channels, but some CB radios may have the capability to access them.

docs.title: Legal Framework in Croatia
docs.text:
CB radio use in Croatia is governed by:

  • Electronic Communications Act (ZEK) — NN 73/08 and amendments
  • Ordinance on conditions and manner of using the radio spectrum — HAKOM
  • European Decision 2006/771/EC and amendment 2013/752/EU — harmonises CB across the EU

Key rules: 1. Devices must carry a CE mark and be type-approved to EN 300 433 2. Modifying or amplifying the device beyond permitted power is prohibited 3. Interfering with emergency communications (Channel 9) is prohibited 4. Broadcasting music, advertisements, or content that interferes with communications is prohibited

Amplifiers (linear amplifiers) are illegal

Amplifiers that raise CB power above 4 W (AM/FM) or 12 W (SSB) are illegal in the EU and Croatia. HAKOM can: - Issue fines - Confiscate equipment - Initiate criminal proceedings for intentional interference

docs.title: International Differences
docs.text:
The CB channel plan is not entirely unified — regional differences exist:

Region Standard Notes Europe (EU) CEPT, 40 channels Identical frequency plan, FM dominant USA FCC, 40 channels Same plan, AM more dominant, same power Australia ACMA, 40 channels Slightly different plan + alpha channels Japan MIC, 40 channels Completely different plan and power

For international DX CB communication, SSB on channels 38–40 is the de facto standard among European operators.

docs.text:

  • Radio Wave Propagation — why CB DX works only occasionally
  • Band Plans — comparison with amateur band plans

docs.title: CB Equipment
docs.text: CB radio equipment is accessible, robust, and requires no special technical knowledge to set up. However, the difference between a poor and a good CB station — especially in terms of range and signal clarity — can be dramatic, and the antenna is almost always the decisive factor.

docs.title: Mobile (vehicle) radios
docs.text:
Mobile CB radios are mounted in vehicles and powered from the 12 V electrical system. This is the most common CB application:

Model Modes Power Size Price (approx.) Notes President McKinley AM/FM/SSB 4/12 W Standard DIN ~€150–200 Popular European choice, SSB, NOAA President Lincoln II+ AM/FM/SSB 4/12 W Wider than DIN ~€200–250 Advanced model, 10 m and CB, SSB Midland Alan 78 Plus AM/FM/SSB 4/12 W Standard DIN ~€120–160 Good value, compact Albrecht AE 6491 AM/FM/SSB 4/12 W Compact ~€130–180 Discreet, control head on cable Midland Alan 48 Plus AM/FM 4 W Mini ~€80–100 No SSB, good for beginners President Bill AM/FM 4 W Mini ~€70–90 Ultra compact

Recommendation for beginners

Midland Alan 48 Plus or President Bill — excellent value for local CB work. If you plan DX or motorway driving with hunters, choose a model with SSB (Alan 78 Plus or McKinley).

docs.title: Base stations
docs.text:
Base stations are installed at home and powered from mains (230 V) or an external 12–13.8 V supply:

  • President George — classic AM/FM/SSB base unit, DIN format, excellent sensitivity
  • Albrecht AE 5890 — modern featuring dual watch, USB charger
  • Older models such as Uniden HR 2510 or Galaxy DX 99V — popular with the DX community for their improved filters

Alternative: mobile radio + power supply

Many users run a mobile radio at home with an inexpensive regulated power supply (13.8 V / 5+ A). The result is identical to a "base station" in performance, at a lower cost.

docs.title: Handheld radios
docs.text:
For hiking, camping, and off-road use:

Model Modes Power Battery Notes President Randy FCC AM/FM 4 W 1,500 mAh Li-Ion IP67 waterproof, robust Midland Alan 42 DS AM/FM 4 W 1,200 mAh Li-Ion Compact, dual band (EU/US) Albrecht AE 33H AM/FM 4 W AA batteries Long runtime

Handheld radios have a shorter range due to the short antenna — typically 2–5 km in open terrain, less in woodland. For convoys and camping this is perfectly adequate.

docs.title: CB Antennas — The Most Important Part of the System
docs.text: On CB, more than any other single factor, the antenna determines your range. The difference between a poor and a good antenna can easily be a 10× improvement in range. Given the fixed 4 W power limit, investing in the antenna always pays off more than illegal power increases.

docs.title: CB Antenna Physics
docs.text:
The wavelength at 27 MHz is approximately 11 metres. A full-wave antenna (dipole) would have arms 5.5 m long — practical for a base station, but not for a car. This is why CB mobile antennas use loading coils to electrically shorten the element.

\[\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{300\,000\,\text{km/s}}{27\,\text{MHz}} \approx 11.1\,\text{m}\]

The closer the antenna is physically to the full resonant length (or \(\lambda/4 \approx 2.75\) m without a coil), the more efficient it is.

docs.title: Mobile (vehicle) antennas
docs.text:
Antenna Length Gain Mount Price Notes Sirio Performer 5000 171 cm ~3 dBd M6/PL stud ~€60–80 Popular high-performance antenna President Virginia 140 cm ~2 dBd Magnetic ~€40–60 Magnetic base, good pattern Midland 1822 Dual Firestik 155 cm ~3 dBd Bracket ~€50–70 Fibreglass, durable Albrecht 6130 120 cm ~1.5 dBd Magnetic ~€25–40 Budget option, ok for local

Rules for mobile antennas: 1. The higher the mount, the better the range — rooftop beats bonnet every time 2. Longer is generally better (to a reasonable limit) — avoid very short antennas 3. Magnetic bases are convenient but have worse RF ground — a bolted mount is always better

docs.title: SWR — Standing Wave Ratio
docs.text:
SWR (Standing Wave Ratio) measures how well the antenna is matched to the transmitter at the operating frequency. Poor SWR means power is reflected back into the transmitter, which can damage it and reduces range.

\[\text{SWR} = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \frac{1 + |\Gamma|}{1 - |\Gamma|}\] SWR value Rating Action 1.0 – 1.5 ✅ Excellent None needed 1.5 – 2.0 ✅ Acceptable Minor adjustment possible 2.0 – 2.5 ⚠️ Poor Antenna adjustment required > 2.5 ❌ Dangerous Stop immediately — transmitter damage likely

Adjusting a CB antenna: Nearly all mobile CB antennas have an adjustable tip or sliding whip for fine-tuning length. Procedure: 1. Connect an SWR meter between the coax cable and antenna 2. Key up on channels 1, 20, and 40 and measure SWR on each 3. Shorten or lengthen the antenna element until SWR is below 1.5 on all channels 4. If SWR is higher at the band edges, the antenna is too long or too short

Never transmit without an antenna connected!

Transmitting with no antenna (or a disconnected antenna connector) can burn out the output transistor within seconds. Always verify the antenna is connected before keying up.

docs.title: Base station antennas
docs.text:
For home base stations on 27 MHz, full-size verticals without loading coils are available:

Antenna Height Gain Price Notes Sirio 827 5.65 m 3 dBd ~€120–180 Popular, EU/US channels Sirio Gain-Master 7.1 m 4 dBd ~€200–280 High gain, premium Albrecht GP 27 CB 5.9 m 3 dBd ~€100–140 Solid budget option President ML145 4.6 m 2 dBd ~€70–100 Height/gain compromise

Base antennas are mounted on a rooftop, mast, or balcony — the higher, the greater the range. Coaxial cable from the base antenna to the radio should be quality cable (RG-8X or RG-213 for longer runs, minimum RG-58 for short runs up to 5 m).

docs.title: Coaxial cables
docs.text: Type Diameter Max. useful length Notes RG-58 5 mm up to 5 m Flexible, higher losses over distance RG-8X 7 mm up to 20 m Flexibility/loss compromise, base station standard RG-213 10 mm up to 50 m Low loss, stiff, professional installations Airspace/Ecoflex Various 100+ m Premium, minimal loss, expensive

docs.title: Essential accessories
docs.text:
• SWR meter — mandatory for antenna tuning (€10–50)
  • PL-259 connectors — standard CB connector; match them to your cable diameter
  • Mounting hardware — required for base antenna installation (mast brackets, clamps)
  • Regulated 13.8 V / 5 A power supply — for using a mobile radio as a base station (€30–80)

docs.title: How to Increase Range (Legally)
docs.text:
Without increasing power, range can be improved by:

  1. Better antenna — higher gain, higher mounting position, better SWR
  2. Shorter and thicker coaxial cable — reduces losses
  3. Mode selection — SSB on channels 38–40 gives 2–3× the range of AM at the same power
  4. Exploit propagation — use Sporadic-E and tropospheric conditions for DX
  5. Improve audio — a good microphone with compression, clear diction, slower speech

The rule: antenna always before power

Doubling the transmit power gives a 3 dB signal increase — barely noticeable in practice. Doubling the antenna gain achieves the same 3 dB, legally and without risk of burning out the transmitter. Investment in the antenna always returns more than illegal power amplification.

docs.title: Introduction to CB Radio
docs.text:
CB (Citizens Band) radio is a public radio service open to everyone — no exam, no special licence. It sits between a walkie-talkie and amateur radio: far more capable than the former, but without the legal obligations of the latter.

In Croatia and across Europe, CB remains a living community — on motorways, in the hills, and among enthusiasts who enjoy simple, licence-free communication.

docs.title: A Brief History
docs.text:
CB radio has its roots in the United States in the 1940s, when the FCC (Federal Communications Commission) allocated part of the spectrum for non-commercial, public communication. The real boom came in the 1970s — CB became a cultural phenomenon:

  • American trucking culture built an entire language and identity around CB ("Smokey and the Bandit", "Convoy")
  • "CB slang" entered popular culture — "breaker breaker", "10-4", "smokey" (police)
  • In Europe, CB spread in the 1980s as a cheap alternative to telephones for drivers and small businesses

The digital revolution and mobile phones reduced CB traffic, but the community has not disappeared — it is still felt on motorways, hiking trails, and off-road tracks.

docs.title: 🚛 Truck Drivers and Transport Workers
docs.text: Channel 19 (27.185 MHz) is the unwritten standard for communication on European motorways. Drivers exchange information about: - Traffic jams and accidents - Speed camera locations - Road conditions (fog, ice, roadworks) - Available truck stops and rest areas

docs.title: 🚙 Off-Road and 4×4 Community
docs.text: CB is the standard in off-road convoys — more reliable than mobile phones in hilly or forested terrain where there is no signal, and convoy coordination is vital for safety.

docs.title: 🏕️ Hiking and Camping
docs.text: Hiking and camping groups use CB for group coordination without call costs. Handheld CB radios are inexpensive and relatively robust.

docs.title: 🛡️ Preppers and Emergency Community
docs.text: CB is popular in the prepper community as an independent communications network that does not depend on infrastructure (mobile network, internet). In the event of a power outage or disaster, CB remains operational.

docs.title: 📻 DX Enthusiasts
docs.text: A smaller but dedicated group hunts long-distance contacts on 27 MHz via Sporadic-E and F2 propagation. This is CB DXing and differs very little from amateur radio DX.

docs.title: Differences from Amateur Radio
docs.text:
CB Radio Amateur Radio Exam ❌ Not required ✅ Technical exam Licence ❌ Not required ✅ National licence Power up to 4 W (AM/FM) up to 1,500 W Frequencies 40 fixed channels entire bands (160 m – 70 cm and more) Operating modes AM, FM, SSB AM, SSB, CW, FM, FT8, satellites… Communication Local/regional + occasional DX Worldwide every day Community Drivers, off-road, preppers, hobbyists Organised, with exams and clubs Entry cost €50–200 €200–1,000+ (depending on goals)

Path from CB to the amateur exam

If you are a CB user curious about more — the logical next step is the amateur radio exam. You already know the fundamentals: modulation, antennas, propagation. The technical knowledge you need is gained by studying for the exam — see How to Become a Radio Amateur.

docs.title: CB Regulations in Croatia
docs.text:
CB radio in Croatia is regulated by HAKOM (Croatian Regulatory Authority for Network Industries). Key regulations:

  • CB radio in the 26.960–27.410 MHz band falls under general authorisation — no special licence required for possession and use.
  • Devices must carry a CE mark and be type-approved for the EU market (EN 300 433 standard).
  • Modifying power output beyond the prescribed limits is prohibited — illegal amplification of CB radios ("turbo" and "linear" amplifiers) can result in fines and equipment confiscation.
  • Channel 9 is reserved for emergencies — it must not be used for normal communication.

Illegal amplifiers (linear amplifiers)

Amplifiers that boost CB power from 4 W to 50–300 W are widespread in the CB community. However, they are: - Illegal — a criminal offence under the Electronic Communications Act - Harmful — such power interferes with adjacent channels, TV reception, and even medical devices - Unnecessary — on 27 MHz a better antenna always delivers more than extra power HAKOM carries out inspections and issues fines.

docs.title: CB Culture and Etiquette
docs.text:
Every radio community has its unwritten rules. CB is no exception:

  • Do not monopolise the channel — exchange briefly, then free the frequency
  • Channel 9 for emergencies only — do not use it for calling friends
  • Identify yourself — though not a legal requirement as in amateur radio, it is courteous
  • No obscenities or offensive content — CB is a public service accessible to everyone, including children
  • Listen before transmitting — always check the frequency is clear

CB \"10-codes\"

The CB community uses 10-codes — abbreviations for common phrases that speed up communication:

Code Meaning 10-4 Received / Understood 10-20 My location is… ("What's your 10-20?") 10-7 Going off the air 10-8 On the air, available 10-36 What is the correct time? 10-33 Emergency!

docs.text:

  • How to Become a Radio Amateur — the next step
  • Radio Wave Propagation — why CB DX works only occasionally

docs.title: Community
docs.text:
Amateur radio is by its very nature a social hobby. Yes, you can sit alone in your shack and make contacts with the whole world — but the full dimension of the hobby only opens up through community: club fellowship, field trips, team competitions, mentoring, youth camps.

At every level — local, national and international — there are organisations that bring amateur radio operators together, protect the interests of the hobby, and organise activities.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Associations and Organisations — HRS, IARU, ARRL, and the structure of the global community
  • Radio Clubs — why to join, what clubs offer, how to find a club in Croatia
  • YOTA — Youngsters On The Air — programme for young amateur radio operators up to age 26

docs.title: Knowledge is transmitted in person
docs.text: Learning amateur radio from a book is possible, but slower. In a club there is always someone who has already "walked the path" — they know which antenna tuner to buy, how to adjust SWR, which software to install for FT8, which contest to recommend for beginners. Mentoring is informal, but worth more than any book.

docs.title: Shared equipment
docs.text: A high-end station — a Yagi on a rotating tower, a linear amplifier, an SDR setup — costs tens of thousands of euros. A club can afford this; an individual rarely can. As a club member, you have access to equipment and infrastructure that would otherwise be out of reach.

docs.title: Exams and licensing
docs.text: In Croatia, amateur radio exams are coordinated by HRS — the association organises exam sessions and provides materials. Many clubs run preparatory courses for candidates.

docs.title: Ham Spirit — the community's unwritten code
docs.text: Almost every amateur radio operator will help you if you ask. Call a DX station and they know you are a Rookie — they will slow down the exchange. Your radio breaks on a SOTA summit — someone will lend you a spare. This culture of mutual support — "ham spirit" — predates the internet and lives in every QSO.

docs.title: Community Structure (Local to Global)
docs.text:
Individual amateur radio operator
        │
        ▼
   Radio club (local)
        │
        ▼
   HRS (Croatian Amateur Radio Association)
        │
        ▼
   IARU (International union)
   ├── Region 1 (Europe, Africa, Middle East)
   ├── Region 2 (Americas)
   └── Region 3 (Asia, Oceania)

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• Associations and Organisations
  • Radio Clubs
  • YOTA
  • How to Become a Radio Amateur

docs.title: Associations and Organisations
docs.text: Amateur radio is regulated and organised at every level — from the local club to an international organisation that represents the hobby before the United Nations. Understanding this structure helps you know where to go for an exam, how to appeal a regulatory decision, or how to get involved in international programmes.

docs.title: HRS — Croatian Amateur Radio Association
docs.text:
HRS (Hrvatski radioamaterski savez — Croatian Amateur Radio Association) is the national umbrella organisation for all amateur radio operators in the Republic of Croatia, founded in 1946. HRS is a registered non-profit association, partially funded through the Ministry (as an association of special public interest) and through membership fees.

Web: www.hamradio.hr

docs.title: What Does HRS Do?
docs.text: Activity Description Exams and licences Organises exam sessions for all amateur licence classes in cooperation with HAKOM QSL bureau Operates the national QSL bureau — receives and forwards cards to/from foreign bureaus Contests Organises or coordinates the 9A DX Contest, 9A Activity Contest, and represents Croatia in CQ WW and other international contests Frequency plan Coordinates with HAKOM on spectrum allocation and protection of amateur frequencies YOTA and youth Coordinates YOTA activities in Croatia, supports young operators ARDF Organises ARG competitions and selects and prepares the team for European and World championships Publications Publishes periodic bulletins and technical materials Representation Represents amateur radio operators before government bodies (HAKOM, Ministry)

docs.title: How to Become a Member of HRS?
docs.text:
Two routes:

  1. Through a radio club — join a local club that is a collective HRS member. This is the recommended route as you also gain all club benefits.
  2. Directly — individual membership is possible without a club. Contact the HRS secretariat.

Annual membership fees for individual members are nominal (check the current amount on the website). A licence only requires passing the exam — HRS membership is not a prerequisite for a licence, but is recommended for access to all services.

docs.title: IARU — International Amateur Radio Union
docs.text:
IARU (International Amateur Radio Union) is the global umbrella organisation representing amateur radio operators before the ITU (International Telecommunication Union — the UN agency regulating radio spectrum use globally).

Web: www.iaru.org

IARU is divided into 3 regions by geography:

Region Area Secretariat Region 1 Europe, Africa, Middle East, northern Asia Geneva, Switzerland Region 2 North and South America — Region 3 Asia and the Pacific —

Croatia (HRS) is a full member of IARU Region 1.

docs.title: What Does IARU Do?
docs.text:
• Represents amateur radio operators at ITU World Radiocommunication Conferences (WRC)
  • Coordinates frequency plans (band plans) within regions — this is the source of the IARU Region 1 band plan we use
  • Defines standards for amateur satellites (AMSAT)
  • Coordinates the ARISS (Amateur Radio on the International Space Station) programme
  • Defends amateur spectrum from commercial pressure

Why IARU matters to you

Every time you see the IARU Region 1 band plan (which segment is for SSB, which for CW, which for digital modes) — that is the result of IARU's work. Without IARU, commercial interests would have long since encroached on the spectrum we use for FT8, SOTA, or satellites.

docs.title: ARRL — American Radio Relay League
docs.text:
ARRL is the US national amateur radio organisation, but has global relevance far beyond the USA:

  • Administers the DXCC programme — the most important award in DXing, accepted globally
  • Manages LoTW (Logbook of the World) — the digital QSO confirmation system
  • Publishes QST magazine and the ARRL Handbook
  • Organises ARRL DX Contest and many other contests

Web: www.arrl.org

As a non-US operator, ARRL is relevant to you for: - Registering for LoTW (free) - DXCC award applications - Technical resources (Antenna Book, Handbook)

docs.title: DARC — Deutscher Amateur Radio Club
docs.text:
DARC is the German amateur radio organisation, one of the largest in Europe with over 40,000 members. Globally known for:

  • WAE DX Contest (Worked All Europe)
  • CQ DL magazine
  • Technical publications and resources

Web: www.darc.de

docs.title: RSGB — Radio Society of Great Britain
docs.text:
Relevant globally for: - IOTA Contest (Islands On The Air) — oldest and one of the most popular portable contests - G-QRP Club — QRP community, DIY projects - Technical publications

Web: www.rsgb.org

docs.title: Other Organisations
docs.text: Organisation Focus Web AMSAT Amateur satellites, OSCAR programme amsat.org SOTA Association Summits On The Air programme sota.org.uk POTA Parks On The Air pota.app IOTA Ltd. Islands On The Air iota-world.org G-QRP Club QRP community gqrp.com LoTW (ARRL) Digital QSL confirmations arrl.org/logbook-of-the-world Club Log Log management, DXCC analysis clublog.org eQSL Alternative digital QSL eqsl.cc

docs.title: Online Community Resources
docs.text:
Beyond formal organisations, these online resources form a coherent global community:

  • QRZ.com — global operator database, callsign lookup, logs, forums
  • DX Summit — DX cluster, real-time spots
  • Reddit r/amateurradio — active English-language forum
  • HamSphere — amateur radio simulator (for practice without a licence)

docs.text:

  • How to Become a Radio Amateur — exam and licence
  • Contesting — HRS competitions

docs.title: Radio Clubs
docs.text: A radio club is the basic cell of the amateur radio community — a place where enthusiasts of all experience levels meet, share knowledge and equipment, organise field trips and competitions. You can join a radio club even without a licence — many people begin as guests or associate members while preparing for their exam.

docs.title: 1. Access to Equipment
docs.text: A serious amateur radio station — HF transceiver, antenna system, linear amplifier, rotator — can cost anywhere from €2,000 to €20,000 or more. A well-equipped club shack is available to all members. Many clubs have: - HF stations with linear amplifiers - Yagi or beam antennas on rotating towers - VHF/UHF repeaters and digipeaters - SDR setups for monitoring - ARDF (direction finding) equipment for training

docs.title: 2. Mentoring and Education
docs.text: Every club has veterans with decades of experience. Informal mentoring is a faster path than self-study: - Exam preparation (many clubs run preparatory courses) - Practical advice on your first station (what to buy, what to avoid) - Guidance on antenna installation, SWR tuning, CAT control - Introduction to specific activities (SOTA, FT8, contesting)

docs.title: 3. Social Aspect
docs.text: Amateur radio can look like a solitary hobby, but in practice the QSO is just one form of interaction. Club meetings, field trips, team competitions — all of this builds friendships that last for decades.

docs.title: 4. Licensing Benefits
docs.text:
Collective membership in HRS through a club brings administrative benefits: - Special club callsigns (e.g. 9A0X, 9A100HRS) available for special occasions - Coordinated participation in contests (Multi-Op categories) - Access to club repeaters and APRS infrastructure

Visit before you join

Almost all clubs welcome guests at club evenings and activities. Come along, meet people, see the equipment and atmosphere — and then decide. The amateur radio community is open and welcoming.

docs.title: How to Find a Club in Croatia?
docs.text:
HRS website: www.hamradio.hr/radioklubovi contains an updated directory of all active radio clubs in Croatia, with contacts and websites.

Some active clubs by region (examples — not exhaustive):

docs.title: Zagreb and surroundings
docs.text: Club Callsign Web / Contact Radioklub Zagreb 9A1RZG 9a1rzg.hr Radioklub Tehničar 9A1T — Radioklub Sesvete 9A1RSE —

docs.title: Primorje and Istria
docs.text: Club Callsign Web / Contact Radioklub Rijeka 9A1RRI — Radioklub Opatija 9A1ROP — Radioklub Pula 9A1RPU —

docs.title: Dalmatia
docs.text: Club Callsign Web / Contact Radioklub Split 9A1RST — Radioklub Dubrovnik 9A1RDU — Radioklub Zadar 9A1RZD —

docs.title: Slavonia and Baranja
docs.text:
Club Callsign Web / Contact Radioklub Osijek 9A1ROS — Radioklub Vukovar 9A1RVU — Radioklub Slavonski Brod 9A1RSB —

Keeping current

Club listings and contacts change — always check hamradio.hr for the latest information.

docs.title: What Happens at Radio Clubs?
docs.text:
Activities vary by club, but typically include:

  • Regular club meetings — weekly or fortnightly, often with technical talks or guest presentations
  • Club activations — S2S, SOTA, POTA, IOTA field trips using the club callsign
  • Contests — Multi-Op teams for CQ WW, 9A DX Contest, and local events
  • Exam courses — preparing candidates for the amateur radio exam
  • Flea markets (hamfest) — annual or seasonal equipment hamfests
  • ARDF training — direction finding under club organisation
  • Antenna workshops — club construction of dipoles, Yagis, delta loops
  • Youth and YOTA — club YOTA activities for operators under 26

docs.title: Club Callsigns and Special Events
docs.text: Radio clubs can obtain a club callsign (e.g. 9A1RZG) for contests and regular operations. In addition, for special occasions (anniversaries, World Amateur Radio Day, YOTA month) HRS assigns special event callsigns with thematic suffixes (e.g. 9A100HRS for the 100th anniversary). DX hunters worldwide chase these callsigns — by participating you contribute to a global amateur radio event.

docs.title: Access for Unlicensed Beginners
docs.text:
Can I join without a licence?

Yes — almost every club accepts people without an amateur licence as associate/guest members. As such, you may listen, practise on equipment under a licensed operator's supervision, and attend activities. You may transmit independently only once you hold a licence. A club is the ideal place to prepare for the exam.

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Associations and Organisations — HRS and IARU
  • YOTA — programme for young operators
  • How to Become a Radio Amateur — exam and licence
  • Contesting — Multi-Op contesting through a club

docs.title: YOTA — Youngsters On The Air
docs.text:
YOTA (Youngsters On The Air) is an international initiative of IARU Region 1 with a clear objective: to attract young people to amateur radio and keep them active in the hobby. The programme is open to operators up to 26 years of age — regardless of whether they already hold a licence or are simply interested.

Croatia is one of the most active YOTA countries in Europe, with regular participation in international camps and excellent competition results.

docs.title: Why Does YOTA Exist?
docs.text:
Amateur radio is a hobby with a tradition of over 100 years — but like any hobby, it needs to attract new generations. IARU recognised that without actively bringing in young people, the average age of operators rises continually. YOTA is the response to that challenge.

The statistic that drives action

In some European countries the average age of a licensed amateur radio operator exceeds 60. The YOTA programme, launched in 2011, is now active in 50+ countries and involves thousands of new young operators every year.

docs.title: YOTA Summer Camp
docs.text:
The most important annual YOTA event — a week-long camp held in a different European country each year:

  • 50–100 young operators from 30+ countries participate
  • Technical amateurs from the host organisation lead workshops and lectures
  • Active club stations, contests, DXCC hunting, antenna building workshops
  • A strong social element — friendships that last for years
  • Accommodation and programme are subsidised (HRS pays or co-funds costs for Croatian representatives)

Croatia has previously hosted the YOTA Summer Camp, and young Croatian operators regularly participate in camps abroad.

docs.title: YOTA Month (December)
docs.text:
Every year in December, hundreds of operators across Region 1 activate special club callsigns with the suffix YOTA (e.g. 9A00YOTA, DL0YOTA, G0YOTA):

  • DX hunters worldwide chase these callsigns — massive interest
  • Young operators staff the stations and log contacts
  • Special certificates for hunters who collect YOTA callsigns

From a hunter's perspective: collect QSOs with YOTA callsigns and submit your log through the YOTA web system.

docs.title: YOTA Contest
docs.text: An amateur radio contest tailored for young people, featuring: - A special category for Rookie and YOTA operators - A simpler exchange (appropriate for beginners) - Promotion of CW mode (Morse telegraphy) among younger generations

docs.title: Other YOTA Activities
docs.text:
• JOTA (Jamboree on the Air) — annual event where Scouts worldwide communicate via amateur radio (always the third weekend of October)
  • Amateur radio in schools — YOTA projects bringing amateur radio operators into schools for demonstrations
  • Mentoring — experienced YOTA alumni mentor new members

docs.title: YOTA in Croatia
docs.text:
The Croatian YOTA community operates through HRS and local radio clubs. Particularly active in:

  • Contests — young Croatian operators regularly place highly in Rookie and YOTA categories
  • SOTA activations — YOTA + SOTA combination is popular; hiking and radio in one
  • ARDF competitions — Croatia's direction finding team has a strong youth component
  • YOTA camp participation — Croatian candidates are selected through HRS and club recommendations

How to get involved with YOTA in Croatia: 1. Contact HRS secretariat or a local YOTA coordinator (hamradio.hr) 2. Join a local radio club with an active youth section 3. Follow YOTA activities at www.ham-yota.com 4. Apply for the summer camp through HRS (places are limited — apply early!)

No licence? No problem!

YOTA is open to people without an amateur licence. At camps and club activities you can participate, listen, and practise under a licensed operator's supervision. YOTA is an excellent motivator for taking the exam.

docs.title: How to Get Started as a Young Amateur Radio Operator
docs.text:
If you are under 26 and amateur radio interests you — here is the shortest path:

  1. Find a local radio club — HRS listing — visit as a guest
  2. Take the exam — the club will help you prepare; the exam is achievable with 2–3 months of study
  3. Get your callsign — and make your first contact!
  4. Sign up for YOTA activities — camps, YOTA month, contests
  5. Try portable radio — SOTA or POTA are excellent for young people who enjoy the outdoors
  6. Learn CW — Morse code is a valuable skill and unlocks a wealth of contest categories

docs.text:
• Radio Clubs — the club as a base for YOTA activities
  • Associations and Organisations — HRS and IARU structure
  • Portable Operations — SOTA/POTA — favourite YOTA field activations
  • ARDF — Fox Hunting — direction finding popular among young operators
  • How to Become a Radio Amateur — exam and licence

docs.title: Emergency Communications (EmComm)
docs.text:
Amateur radio operators are often the last line of communication when infrastructure fails — earthquakes, floods, violent storms, or cyberattacks can knock out mobile networks, internet, and telephone exchanges in minutes. In these situations an amateur radio operator with a battery, an antenna, and a transceiver becomes a vital link in the rescue chain.

EmComm (Emergency Communications) is not a separate branch of amateur radio, but rather the application of every skill you already have — knowledge of frequencies, propagation, operating procedures, digital modes — in the context of critical situations.

EmComm is not a game

Working in emergencies requires discipline, training, and coordination with competent authorities. Improvisation and unauthorised transmissions can complicate rescue operations. Operators wishing to do EmComm work should educate themselves and register with structured programmes (RMZO, ARES, RACES).

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• RMZO — Croatian Amateur Radio Emergency Network
  • Emergency Procedures — radio discipline, message priorities, standard formats
  • Winlink — email without the internet: digital messages via radio

docs.title: Independence from infrastructure
docs.text:
Commercial systems (GSM, 4G/5G, internet) depend on: - The electrical grid - Fibre-optic cables - Cell towers - Server centres

All of this can disappear or become overloaded within the first hour of a disaster. An amateur radio station with a generator or solar battery continues to operate independently.

docs.title: Range without borders
docs.text:
• VHF/UHF repeaters (if they have backup power) cover locally and regionally
  • HF shortwave signals cross continents without any infrastructure at all
  • Satellite modes (on amateur satellites) operate independently of ground networks

docs.title: Verified operators
docs.text: Licensed amateur radio operators are verified individuals who know the regulations, operating protocols, and frequency etiquette. This distinguishes them from unregulated CB users or improvised solutions.

docs.title: IARU EmComm
docs.text: IARU coordinates the global EmComm network through: - Standardised EmComm frequencies by region - IARU Emergency Telecommunications Guide - Coordination with international organisations (IFRC, UN-OCHA, WHO)

docs.title: ARES (Amateur Radio Emergency Service)
docs.text: The largest voluntary EmComm programme, originally American (ARRL) but now global. Operators register with their local ARES group, undergo training, and make themselves available to local emergency services.

docs.title: RACES (Radio Amateur Civil Emergency Service)
docs.text: US programme that integrates amateur radio operators directly into civil defence and government structures.

docs.title: EmComm in Practice — Historical Examples
docs.text: Disaster Country Role of amateur radio Petrinja earthquake (2020) Croatia RMZO activation, coordination with DUZS Hurricane Katrina (2005) USA ARES activation, only communications in parts of New Orleans Turkish earthquakes (2023) Türkiye TRAC, coordination with UN agencies COVID-19 coordination (2020) EU Winlink networks for medical supply logistics Slavonia floods (2014) Croatia VHF/HF radio networks for evacuation coordination

docs.title: How to Prepare
docs.text:
1. Register with RMZO — contact HRS or a local radio club
  2. Learn EmComm procedures — see Emergency Procedures
  3. Set up a portable station — a radio that runs on battery or solar, independent of mains power
  4. Learn Winlink — Winlink guide for digital messages without internet
  5. Practise regularly — EmComm skills need regular application (club drills, Field Day)

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• RMZO
  • Emergency Procedures
  • Winlink
  • Portable Operations — portable station as the basis of an EmComm setup
  • Safety — safe setup in crisis conditions

docs.title: Emergency Procedures
docs.text:
In emergency communications, discipline saves lives. The difference between chaotic and coordinated radio communications in a disaster situation can be decisive for the timely response of rescue services.

This page covers the fundamental principles, standard message formats, and rules of conduct on an EmComm net.

docs.title: 1. Listening is primary
docs.text:
Before you transmit anything — listen. Find out: - Is the frequency already in use? - Is there a Net Control Station (NCS)? - What type of traffic is there — routine, urgent, priority?

Rule: 90% of the time you listen, 10% you transmit.

docs.title: 2. Short, clear, no jargon
docs.text: EmComm communication is not a nuanced conversation — it is the transmission of information under stress. Use: - Standard phrases (not improvisation) - Phonetic alphabet for spelling critical data - Short sentences — one message, one idea - Numbers spoken distinctly — "one-zero-two" not "one hundred and two"

docs.title: 3. Write everything down
docs.text:
Every QSO in EmComm must be documented: - UTC time - Callsign of the sender - Message content - Action taken / forwarded to whom

A paper log is a backup when the laptop fails. Always have a notebook and pen.

docs.title: 4. Do not transmit without NCS permission
docs.text:
When a Net Control Station is operating, all communication goes through the NCS. Do not call other stations directly, do not relay messages without announcing, do not check into the net and immediately start transmitting.

Frequency stepping = chaos

Simultaneous transmission by multiple stations (carrier clash) results in an unintelligible signal and a lost message. In an EmComm situation that means lost information about casualties or locations. Maintain discipline.

docs.title: Message Priority
docs.text:
Messages are classified by urgency:

Priority Name Description Example 🔴 1 DISTRESS / MAYDAY Immediate danger to life "Mayday, mayday — vessel sinking, 12 persons on board" 🟠 2 URGENCY / PAN-PAN Urgent situation, no immediate threat to life "Pan-Pan — passenger with cardiac arrest, need ambulance at location X" 🟡 3 SAFETY / SÉCURITÉ Warning of danger to others "Sécurité — earthquake recorded, possible road damage on route Y" 🟢 4 WELFARE Status information about casualties / injured List of evacuees, infrastructure condition ⚪ 5 ROUTINE Routine coordination and logistics Frequency change, station status, relay request

docs.title: MAYDAY call
docs.text:
Use exclusively for immediate danger to life:

MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY
This is [callsign]
Mayday — [description of situation in 1-2 sentences]
Our position is [coordinates or location description]
There are [number of persons] at this location
We require [food/water/medical aid/evacuation]
[callsign], over

docs.title: PAN-PAN call
docs.text:
For urgent situations without immediate threat to life:

PAN-PAN PAN-PAN PAN-PAN
All stations, this is [callsign]
Pan-Pan — [description of situation]
Location: [description]
[Request for assistance]
[callsign], over

docs.title: IARU Standard EmComm Message
docs.text:
For structured transmission of information through the net (written — Winlink or relay):

1. PRECEDENCE: [Routine / Welfare / Priority / Emergency]
2. FROM: [callsign]
3. TO: [callsign or institution]
4. DATE/TIME (UTC): [DD/MMM/YYYY HH:MM]
5. RECEIVED VIA: [relay station callsigns]
6. MESSAGE:
   [Clear, brief message. One idea per sentence.
    Do not use abbreviations the recipient may not know.]
7. END OF MESSAGE
8. [sender's callsign]

Example IARU EmComm message

1. PRECEDENCE: Priority
2. FROM: 9A7YY
3. TO: 9A1XX (NCS)
4. DATE/TIME: 29/DEC/2024 14:35 UTC
5. RECEIVED VIA: Direct
6. MESSAGE:
   Reporting from location Petrinja, Moslavačka Street.
   Building at number 22 partially collapsed, I can see 3 survivors.
   Access difficult — street blocked by rubble.
   Coordinates: 45.4417 N, 16.2744 E.
   Heavy machinery required for access.
7. END OF MESSAGE
8. 9A7YY

docs.title: Phonetic Alphabet (ICAO/NATO)
docs.text:
In EmComm communication, every letter that could be misheard must be spelled using the phonetic alphabet:

Letter NATO Letter NATO A Alpha N November B Bravo O Oscar C Charlie P Papa D Delta Q Quebec E Echo R Romeo F Foxtrot S Sierra G Golf T Tango H Hotel U Uniform I India V Victor J Juliet W Whiskey K Kilo X X-ray L Lima Y Yankee M Mike Z Zulu

Numbers are spoken clearly: "niner" for 9 (to avoid confusion with German "nein"), "fife" for 5 (clearly distinct from "fire").

docs.title: Special Rules on EmComm Frequencies
docs.text:
• Never test your antenna by transmitting on an EmComm frequency — it interferes with the net
  • Do not use the frequency for routine contacts except when called by NCS
  • If you hear a MAYDAY — stop transmitting, listen, and write down the information
  • Relay responsibility — if you hear a distress call and NCS has not acknowledged it, it is your duty to act

Legal aspect

Rendering assistance to persons in distress is a moral and in some jurisdictions a legal obligation for radio operators. Ignoring a MAYDAY you received without taking action is not acceptable.

docs.title: Training and Preparation
docs.text:
EmComm skills fade without regular practice. Recommended minimum:

  • Weekly: Check in to the local EmComm net — just confirm your presence
  • Monthly: Club EmComm exercise with message traffic
  • Annually: Field Day (deploying a portable station without mains power)

docs.text:

docs.title: RMZO — Croatian Amateur Radio Emergency Network
docs.text:
RMZO (Radioamaterska mreža za opasnost — Amateur Radio Emergency Network) is an organised system within the Croatian Amateur Radio Association that is activated in the event of natural disasters, technical accidents or any situation where standard telecommunications networks are not functioning.

RMZO is not just a list of frequencies — it is an organisational structure that defines who coordinates whom, which frequencies are used, what message priorities apply, and how reports are submitted to the competent authorities.

docs.title: Legal Framework
docs.text:
RMZO operates in cooperation with: - HAKOM — Croatian Regulatory Authority for Network Industries (assigns special EmComm frequencies) - DUZS/MUP Civil Protection Directorate — government civil protection authority - HRS — coordinates and leads RMZO at the national level - 112 — integration with the national emergency call centre

The Electronic Communications Act gives priority to communications involving danger to life and property over all other traffic.

docs.title: Organisational Structure
docs.text:
HRS (national coordination)
        │
        ▼
Regional coordinators (by county)
        │
        ▼
Local radio clubs and RMZO operators
        │
        ▼
Field stations (mobile and portable)

Roles in RMZO:

Role Description Net Control Station (NCS) Manages the net, grants permission to transmit, logs all traffic Relay station Forwards traffic between stations that cannot hear each other directly Field station Reports from the disaster location or coordination centre Base station Fixed RMZO station, often a club shack with backup power

docs.title: RMZO Frequencies
docs.text:
Frequencies may change

Always verify current RMZO frequencies at hamradio.hr as they may be updated by HRS and HAKOM decisions. Keep a current list in your portable station.

docs.title: HF frequencies (long range, no infrastructure)
docs.text: Frequency Band Purpose 3.675 MHz 80 m Primary national EmComm frequency for Croatia 7.060 MHz 40 m Alternative for daytime work (better daytime propagation) 14.300 MHz 20 m IARU Region 1 EmComm frequency (international)

docs.title: VHF/UHF frequencies (local range, clarity)
docs.text: Frequency Band Purpose 145.500 MHz 2 m Primary local simplex EmComm frequency 144.800 MHz 2 m APRS (automatic packet reporting) 433.500 MHz 70 cm UHF simplex working Local repeaters 2 m / 70 cm Coordination if backup power is available

docs.title: How is the network activated?
docs.text:
RMZO can be activated in two ways:

  1. Official request — DUZS/MUP/112 contacts the HRS coordinator and requests activation
  2. Self-initiated — if it is obvious that communications infrastructure has failed (earthquake, flood, widespread power outage), amateur radio operators can self-organise without waiting for a request

In practice, major disasters trigger both mechanisms simultaneously.

docs.title: Activation Phases
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Monitoring: Operators listen on EmComm frequencies and monitor the situation without transmitting unless they have urgent information.

Phase 2 — Net activation: NCS calls the net and takes over coordination. All stations report their location and availability.

Phase 3 — Operational work: Field stations send reports. NCS filters information and forwards it to the competent authorities.

Phase 4 — Deactivation: NCS announces the end of the operation. All traffic is archived.

docs.title: Example Net Opening
docs.text:
NCS: "All stations, all stations — this is 9A1XX, Net Control for the RMZO EmComm net.
      Opening EmComm net on frequency 3.675 MHz at [UTC]. Mode: SSB.
      Stations with urgent traffic — respond immediately.
      All other stations — respond in callsign order when called.
      9A1XX, standing by."

Field station: "9A1XX, this is 9A7YY from Petrinja, I have a Priority report."

NCS: "9A7YY, you have clearance — go ahead with your report."

docs.title: EmComm Go-Bag Contents
docs.text:
A go-bag is a bag or rucksack with equipment ready for rapid field deployment:

Item Notes Dual-band handheld (VHF/UHF) Charged battery + spare HF QRP transceiver If available (IC-705, KX2, FT-818) LiFePO4 battery 10+ Ah Independent power source Solar panel 20–30 W For extended operation Wire antenna (EFHW or dipole) 10–15 m of wire, connector, short mast Laptop/tablet with Winlink Express Digital messaging VARA modem or SignaLink Audio interface for digital modes Log notebook and pens Paper log always as backup EmComm frequency list Laminated, in the bag Power adapters 12 V and 230 V versions

Test your go-bag regularly

Once per quarter: unpack the equipment, verify batteries are charged, make a short QSO as a test. Equipment that hasn't been tested is not reliable equipment.

docs.title: Field Day — Annual Exercise
docs.text:
Field Day is an annual EmComm exercise — amateur radio operators set up portable stations without mains power and make as many QSOs as possible in 24 or 48 hours. The goal is to test: - Portable equipment - Backup power - Operating procedures - Team coordination

In the USA this is organised by ARRL every June. In Europe and Croatia, clubs organise their own Field Day activities — follow the HRS calendar and club websites.

docs.title: Winlink — Email Without the Internet
docs.text:
Winlink is a global network that enables sending and receiving email messages via radio, without relying on internet infrastructure. In crisis situations — when telephone networks and the internet are unavailable — Winlink remains operational as long as there is a radio signal.

Winlink is used by emergency services, the Red Cross, Coast Guard, ARES teams, and thousands of amateur radio operators for regular digital communication and EmComm exercises.

docs.title: How Winlink Works
docs.text:
The Winlink system has three elements:

[Your computer + radio]
        │
        │ (RF signal — HF or VHF/UHF)
        ▼
[Gateway / RMS station]
(amateur radio station connected to the internet)
        │
        │ (internet)
        ▼
[CMS — Winlink Central Message Server]
(cloud system that stores and forwards messages)
        │
        ├── → Winlink users (retrieve via radio or web)
        └── → Regular internet email recipients

Key advantage: Your message travels by radio to a gateway station that has internet access. You have no internet — but the gateway does, and it forwards the message onwards. Via HF (shortwave) you can connect to a gateway in another country thousands of kilometres away — even if all local infrastructure is completely down.

docs.title: Winlink Operating Modes
docs.text:
Winlink supports several data transmission modes:

VARA HFVARA FMPactorARDOPPacket (AX.25)

The most modern HF mode, high throughput, robust in difficult conditions. - Frequency range: HF (3–30 MHz) - Software: VARA HF modem (free for 1,200 bps, paid for full speed) - Recommended speed: up to 8,400 bps in good conditions - Recommended for EmComm HF work

FM version for VHF/UHF repeaters and simplex frequencies. - Frequency range: VHF/UHF (144 MHz, 432 MHz) - Fast and reliable for local connections - Excellent for coordination within a town or region - Recommended for local EmComm VHF work

Older but robust HF mode, popular in maritime and ARES communities. - Requires a hardware Pactor TNC modem (SCS, ~€400–600) - Excellent throughput for HF, especially on DX links - Used in maritime and expedition stations

Free open-source HF protocol, somewhat slower than VARA but with no licensing cost. - Good option for beginners without the VARA licence cost - Built into Winlink Express

Classic VHF packet radio, reliable for local networks. - In use since the 1980s, well proven - 1,200 bps (or 9,600 bps on UHF) - Foundation for the APRS infrastructure

docs.title: Software — Winlink Express
docs.text:
Winlink Express is the free client software for Windows that integrates all Winlink functionality:

  • Download: winlink.org/downloads
  • Free for amateur radio operators (register with your callsign)
  • Supports all Winlink modes (VARA, ARDOP, Packet, Telnet)
  • Built-in message editor similar to an email client
  • ICS forms — standardised civil protection forms for EmComm

Winlink Express on Linux/Mac

Winlink via the Pat client (free, open-source) is available on macOS and Linux: getpat.io. Alternatively, Winlink Express runs under Wine on Linux.

docs.title: Minimum configuration (VHF/UHF)
docs.text: Component Example Price Computer / laptop Older Windows laptop is fine — VHF/UHF transceiver Icom IC-2300H, Yaesu FT-8900R €100–200 SignaLink USB Audio interface radio-PC ~€90–110 Radio cable Specific to transceiver model €10–30

docs.title: Advanced configuration (HF with VARA)
docs.text:
Component Example Price HF transceiver Icom IC-7300, Yaesu FT-991A €700–1,500 VARA HF licence For full speed (optional) ~$69 CAT cable USB-Serial for CAT control €10–30 SignaLink USB Audio interface ~€90–110

Icom IC-7300 and built-in sound card

The Icom IC-7300 has a built-in USB audio interface and CAT — you connect a single USB cable to the computer and Winlink Express recognises the radio without a SignaLink adapter. An ideal combination for Winlink.

docs.title: ICS Forms in Winlink
docs.text:
ICS (Incident Command System) forms are standardised forms used by emergency services worldwide to document and transmit critical information. Winlink Express embeds ICS forms in digital format:

Form Purpose ICS-213 General Message ICS-214 Activity Log ICS-309 Communications Log Red Cross DDS American Red Cross disaster damage report HICS-215A Hospital communications form

In an EmComm situation, a hospital, shelter, or coordination centre can receive an ICS-213 form sent wirelessly via Winlink and process it as a structured document — without re-entering the same information manually.

docs.title: Winlink Gateway Network in the Croatian Region
docs.text:
Regional HF and VHF Winlink gateways accessible from Croatia:

Station Country Mode Frequency 9A5ST-10 Croatia Pactor/VARA HF (verify at winlink.org) OE stations Austria VARA HF Multiple HF frequencies I stations Italy VARA HF Multiple HF frequencies S5 stations Slovenia VARA FM / HF VHF + HF

For the current gateway list and frequencies, use the map at winlink.org/map.

docs.title: Practical Test — Sending a Test Message
docs.text:
Before you need Winlink in a real EmComm situation, practise by sending test messages:

  1. Install Winlink Express and register (your amateur callsign is the login)
  2. Configure your SignaLink or CAT connection to the radio
  3. Select a mode (for starters: Telnet if you have internet — to test without radio)
  4. Send a message to WLNK-1@winlink.org — a bot that responds with a confirmation
  5. Check your inbox for the reply

Once the test mode works, repeat the same process without internet (VARA FM or HF) — that is the real test.

Monthly Winlink exercise

Every first Sunday of the month, a global Winlink exercise encourages operators to send a test EmComm message. Details at winlink.org. By participating you practise the procedure and your station remains "visible" in the system.

docs.text:

  • Portable Operations — portable station for EmComm
  • Operating Modes — digital modes in general

docs.title: Operating
docs.text: Here you will learn how to actually make contacts, which frequencies to use, and what rules of conduct apply on the air.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Band Plans: Frequency allocation by bands.
  • Modes: Detailed overview of analog and digital modes.
  • QSO Procedures: How to start and end a contact, Q-Codes.
  • Logbooks and QSL Cards: Confirming contacts.

docs.title: Band Plans
docs.text:
A band plan is an agreement on the use of parts of amateur bands for different modes of operation. Respecting the band plan is a matter of amateur radio ethics and culture.

Here we present the plan for IARU Region 1 (Europe), with notes for Region 2 (North & South America) where significant differences exist.

docs.title: 160m (1.8 MHz)
docs.text:
"Top Band". Night band.

Frequency (kHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 1810 - 1838 CW USA: 1800 - 2000 kHz 1838 - 1840 Digital (FT8 @ 1840) 1840 - 2000 SSB / CW

docs.title: 80m (3.5 MHz)
docs.text:
Most popular band for local contacts.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 3.500 - 3.570 CW 3.570 - 3.600 Digital (FT8 @ 3.573) 3.600 - 3.800 SSB (LSB) USA goes up to 4.000 MHz! (Phone: 3.600 - 4.000)

docs.title: 40m (7 MHz)
docs.text:
Reliable band for Europe and DX.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 7.000 - 7.040 CW 7.040 - 7.060 Digital (FT8 @ 7.074) 7.060 - 7.200 SSB (LSB) USA goes up to 7.300 MHz! (Phone starts at 7.125)

docs.title: 20m (14 MHz)
docs.text:
"King of DX".

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 14.000 - 14.070 CW 14.070 - 14.100 Digital (FT8 @ 14.074) 14.100 - 14.350 SSB (USB) IARU R2 is the same.

docs.title: 2m (144 MHz)
docs.text:
Most popular VHF band.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 144.000 - 144.150 CW / SSB (DX) 144.150 - 144.400 SSB / MGM 144.800 APRS USA: APRS is on 144.390 145.000 - 145.175 Repeaters (Input) USA band goes to 148 MHz! 145.200 - 145.575 FM Simplex 145.500 FM Calling Channel USA: 146.520 145.600 - 145.775 Repeaters (Output) Shift is the same (-600 kHz)

docs.title: 70cm (432 MHz)
docs.text: Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 432.000 - 432.400 CW / SSB USA band: 420 - 450 MHz 433.000 - 434.000 FM Simplex / Repeaters 446.000 - 446.200 PMR446 (License-free) USA: FRS/GMRS (462/467 MHz)

docs.title: Logbooks and QSL Cards
docs.text: Every contact made should be logged. This is a legal requirement and part of the tradition.

docs.title: Logbook
docs.text: The logbook must contain: - Date and time (UTC). - Correspondent's call sign. - Frequency (or band). - Mode. - Report (RST).

docs.title: Paper vs. Electronic
docs.text: Today, most use computer programs (loggers) such as N1MM, DXKeeper, or Ham Radio Deluxe. They automatically record time and frequency (if connected to the radio).

docs.title: QSL Cards
docs.text: A QSL card is a written confirmation of a contact. It is the "final courtesy" of a ham. - Paper QSL: Sent directly by mail or (cheaper) via the national association's QSL bureau. - Electronic QSL: - LoTW (Logbook of The World): ARRL system. Officially recognized for awards (DXCC). - eQSL: Popular, but less "official". - ClubLog: Excellent for analysis and OQRS (Online QSL Request System).

docs.title: Modes
docs.text: Radio amateurs use different types of modulation to transmit information. Each mode has its pros, cons, and specific "sound".

docs.title: CW (Continuous Wave / Telegraphy)
docs.text: The oldest form of communication. Uses Morse code (dots and dashes). * Pros: Range (gets through when nothing else can), simple equipment, narrow bandwidth. * Sound: Rhythmic beeping. * Listen to CW (Video)

docs.title: SSB (Single Sideband)
docs.text: The most popular mode for voice on Shortwave (HF). An advanced version of AM that uses less energy. * LSB (Lower Sideband): Used below 10 MHz (40m, 80m). * USB (Upper Sideband): Used above 10 MHz (20m, 17m...) and on VHF/UHF. * Sound: Human voice, but sounds like "Donald Duck" if you are not exactly on frequency. * Listen to SSB (Video)

docs.title: FM (Frequency Modulation)
docs.text: Standard for local contacts on VHF/UHF (handhelds). * Pros: Clear sound, no noise (if signal is strong). * Cons: Requires wide bandwidth, shorter range compared to SSB/CW.

docs.title: Digital Modes (FT8, RTTY...)
docs.text: Computer and radio working together. * FT8: Currently the most popular mode. Allows contacts with very low power and inaudible signals. The contact is automated. * RTTY (Teletype): Older digital mode, sounds like an "80s video game". * FT8 Sound: Sounds like multiple tones warbling. * Listen to FT8 (Video)

docs.title: Waterfall Display
docs.text: Modern radios and software display signals visually. * X Axis: Frequency. * Y Axis: Time. * Color: Signal strength. * Allows you to "see" stations before you hear them.

docs.title: QSO Procedures
docs.text: Making a contact has its rules to ensure efficient and understandable communication.

docs.title: Basic Terms
docs.text:
• CQ: General call ("Calling Any Station").
  • DE: From (used in CW or digital modes).
  • K: Over.
  • 73: Best Regards.

docs.title: Example Contact (SSB)
docs.text:
1. Call: "CQ CQ CQ, this is 9A1AA, 9A1AA calling and listening."
  2. Reply: "9A1AA, this is DL1BBB."
  3. Report: "DL1BBB, good day. Your signal is 59 (five-nine), name is Ivan, locator JN75. 9A1AA."
  4. Confirmation: "9A1AA, thanks Ivan. Your signal is also 59, my name is Hans. 73!"
  5. End: "Thanks Hans, 73 and hope to hear you again. DL1BBB de 9A1AA."

docs.title: RST System
docs.text:
The report consists of 3 digits (for CW) or 2 (for SSB): - R (Readability): 1-5. - S (Signal Strength): 1-9. - T (Tone): 1-9 (CW only).

Example: 599 (Perfect signal on CW).

docs.title: Q-Codes
docs.text: Q-codes are standardized three-letter codes starting with the letter Q. Originally developed for telegraphy to speed up communication, they are now also used in voice modes.

docs.title: Frequently Used Q-Codes
docs.text: Code Question Answer / Advice QRA What is the name of your station? The name of my station is... QRG What is my exact frequency? Your exact frequency is ... kHz. QRL Are you busy? I am busy (please do not interfere). QRM Is my transmission being interfered with? You are being interfered with (1-5). QRN Are you troubled by static? I am troubled by static (1-5). QRO Shall I increase power? Increase power. QRP Shall I decrease power? Decrease power. QRT Shall I stop sending? Stop sending. QRZ Who is calling me? You are being called by... (on ... kHz). QSB Are my signals fading? Your signals are fading. QSL Can you acknowledge receipt? I am acknowledging receipt. QSO Can you communicate with...? I can communicate with... QSY Shall I change to transmission on another frequency? Change to transmission on ... kHz. QTH What is your position in latitude and longitude (or by any other indication)? My position is...

docs.title: All Q-Codes (Amateur Radio)
docs.text: Code Meaning QRA Name of station / Address QRB Distance between stations QRC Who pays the charges (commercial, rare in ham) QRD Where are you bound and where are you from? QRE Estimated time of arrival? QRF Are you returning? QRG Exact frequency QRH Does my frequency vary? QRI Tone of transmission (T in RST) QRJ Are you receiving me badly? QRK Readability of signals (R in RST) QRL Frequency is busy QRM Interference from other stations QRN Atmospheric interference (static) QRO High power QRP Low power QRQ Send faster QRR Are you ready for automatic operation? QRRS Land SOS (rarely used) QRS Send slower QRT Stop sending / Shutting down QRU Have you anything for me? QRV I am ready QRW Shall I inform...? QRX Wait a moment QRY What is my turn? QRZ Who is calling me? QSA Signal strength (S in RST) QSB Fading QSD Keying is defective QSK Can you hear me between your signals (Break-in)? QSL Acknowledge receipt (card) QSM Shall I repeat the last message? QSN Did you hear me? QSO Contact QSP Will you relay to...? QSQ Have you a doctor? QSR Shall I repeat the call? QSS What working frequency will you use? QST General call to all amateurs (message) QSU Shall I send or reply on this frequency? QSV Shall I send a series of V's? QSW Will you send on this frequency? QSX Will you listen to...? QSY Change frequency QSZ Shall I send each word twice? QTA Shall I cancel message? QTB Do you agree with my counting of words? QTC How many messages have you to send? QTH Location (City, Locator) QTR Exact time

docs.title: Regulations & Licensing
docs.text: Here you can find all information related to legal regulations and the process of obtaining an amateur radio license.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• How to become a Ham?: Information on exams for P and A classes.
  • Croatian Regulations: Laws and regulations by HAKOM and HRS.
  • International Regulations: CEPT and IARU recommendations.
  • Call Signs: Structure and allocation of call signs in Croatia.

docs.title: Useful Documents

docs.title: Call Signs
docs.text: A call sign is a unique identifier for an amateur radio station.

docs.title: Structure (Croatia)
docs.text:
Croatian call signs consist of three parts:

  1. Prefix: 9A (indicates Croatia).
  2. Number: 1 - 9 (historically indicated region, today mostly arbitrary but often follows: 1-Zagreb, 2-Dalmatia, 3-Istria/Littoral, 4-Slavonia...). 0 is used for special signs.
  3. Suffix: 1, 2, or 3 letters.
    • 1 letter: Clubs with long tradition or special signs (e.g., 9A1A).
    • 2 letters: Standard personal signs (e.g., 9A2AA) or clubs.
    • 3 letters: Standard personal signs (e.g., 9A3AAA).

docs.title: Types of Call Signs
docs.text:
• Personal Call Signs: Assigned to individuals after passing the exam.
  • Club Call Signs: Assigned to radio clubs.
  • Special Call Signs: For contests, anniversaries, or expeditions (e.g., 9A80Z, 9A0HQ).

docs.title: Identification
docs.text: During operation, a ham must regularly transmit their call sign (at the start, end, and every 10-15 minutes during contact). When operating from another country (CEPT), place that country's prefix before your call sign (e.g., S5/9A1AA if in Slovenia). When operating portable/mobile, add the suffix /P (portable), /M (mobile), or /MM (maritime mobile).

docs.title: Croatian Regulations
docs.text: Amateur radio in Croatia is regulated by a series of laws and ordinances.

docs.title: 1. Electronic Communications Act
docs.text: The fundamental law governing the use of the radio frequency spectrum.

docs.title: 2. Ordinance on Amateur Radio Communications
docs.text: This is the "bible" for hams in Croatia. It defines: - License types and classes. - Frequency bands and power limits. - Technical conditions for stations. - Call sign formation rules.

docs.title: 3. National Table of Frequency Allocations
docs.text: The document that determines the purpose of each part of the spectrum in Croatia.

docs.title: Competent Authorities
docs.text:
• HAKOM (Croatian Regulatory Authority for Network Industries): Issues licenses, allocates call signs, and monitors operation.
    • Web: www.hakom.hr
  • HRS (Croatian Amateur Radio Association): The national association representing the interests of hams, organizing exams, and managing the QSL bureau.
    • Web: www.hamradio.hr

docs.title: How to Become a Ham?
docs.text: The path to an amateur radio license involves passing an examination. In Croatia, there are two classes of amateur radio operators: P (Entry Level) and A (Advanced).

docs.title: Class P (Entry Level)
docs.text: This is the entry point into the world of amateur radio. - Privileges: Operation on all amateur frequency bands, but with limited power (up to 100W on HF, up to 50W on VHF/UHF). - Exam: Simpler, focused on the basics of regulations, technical topics, and operating procedures. - Recommendation: Ideal for beginners who want to get on the air quickly.

docs.title: Class A (Advanced)
docs.text: Full amateur radio privileges. - Privileges: Operation with maximum legal power (up to 1.5 kW in some cases) and access to all international privileges (CEPT HAREC). - Exam: More comprehensive, includes deeper knowledge of electronics and radio theory. - Recommendation: For those who want to utilize the full potential of the hobby.

docs.title: Examination Process
docs.text:
1. Preparation:
    • Find the nearest radio club (Club List). Clubs often organize courses.
    • Download manuals and question pools from the HRS website.
  2. Application:
    • Exams are organized in radio clubs or directly at the HRS.
  3. The Exam:
    • Consists of a written part (test). There is no longer a practical part (Morse code is not mandatory).

docs.title: After the Exam
docs.text:
After successfully passing the exam, you receive an Examination Certificate. With this, you apply to HAKOM for a License to use RF spectrum and the allocation of a call sign.

Tip

The best way to learn is with a mentor in a radio club. Practical experience is invaluable!

docs.title: International Regulations
docs.text: Amateur radio is a global hobby, so international coordination is crucial.

docs.title: ITU (International Telecommunication Union)
docs.text: The UN agency that coordinates the use of the radio frequency spectrum globally. - Radio Regulations (RR): International regulations defining the amateur service.

docs.title: IARU (International Amateur Radio Union)
docs.text: Represents the interests of radio amateurs before the ITU. - Divided into 3 regions: 1. Region 1: Europe, Africa, Middle East, Northern Asia. 2. Region 2: The Americas. 3. Region 3: Rest of Asia, Australia, Oceania.

docs.title: CEPT (European Conference of Postal and Telecommunications Administrations)
docs.text: European organization that harmonizes regulations. - Recommendation T/R 61-01: Allows hams with a CEPT license (Class A in Croatia) to operate in other signatory countries without additional bureaucracy (up to 3 months). - Recommendation T/R 61-02: Harmonized Amateur Radio Examination Certificate (HAREC).

docs.title: What does this mean for you?
docs.text: If you pass Class A in Croatia, your license is recognized in most European countries (and many others). You can travel and operate your station using the prefix of the country you are visiting (e.g., DL/9A1AA).

docs.text: Useful links, tools, and software for radio amateurs.
